title: AI Foundations — From Math to Modern Red Teaming
description: A spiral curriculum that takes a curious learner from a one-sentence mental model of an LLM, through the math behind gradients and transformers, into the engineering of building and serving frontier models, and onward into the adversarial art of breaking them. Every theoretical concept is paired with its production application and its failure mode — the unicorn rule, generalized beyond cyber. Pedagogy: hybrid spiral (project-first hook + thresholds re-encountered at increasing depth), with deliberate practice, retrieval, and dual-coding. Built around 6 threshold concepts: tokens not words, embeddings as coordinates, attention as soft lookup, the loss is the model, training is search, the base model is not the assistant.

modules.title: Mental Models — The Threshold Concepts
modules.objective: Hold the 6 mental models that survive forgetting every line of code. If a learner can explain these to a 12-year-old using only the word 'autocomplete,' they've crossed the first threshold of understanding modern AI.

topics.title: An LLM Is Two Files: The Weights and The Runner
concepts: An LLM shipped to users is essentially two artifacts: a binary blob of neural-network weights (billions of parameters) and a small program (the runner) that loads the weights, feeds in tokens, and samples a next token.
Karpathy's reduction: 'an LLM is two files — model.params and run.c' — captures the entire practical surface area.
Pretraining produces the weights; the runner is the same loop repeated: tokenize → forward pass through the network → next-token logits → softmax → sample a token → append → repeat.
Everything else — chatbots, agents, RAG, fine-tuning — is orchestration around this loop.
Training compresses the internet into weights; inference replays that compression one token at a time.
key_facts: Weights are typically a binary dump of every parameter tensor in the network — for a 70B model in BF16, ~140GB.
The runner is a few hundred lines of code that does tokenize → forward → argmax/sample → repeat.
What the model 'knows' is strictly in the weights; the runner has no idea whether it is answering about microbiology or making a sandwich.
At inference time, the prompt is the only thing you control — every ounce of capability comes from getting it right.
Karpathy's 2023 'Intro to Large Language Models' (1h) builds this picture in the first 10 minutes.
drills: Explain to a non-technical friend what an LLM is using only the words 'autocomplete', 'weights', and 'pattern'.
List the three things every inference call needs: model weights, a tokenizer, and a sampler.
Convince yourself the model has no memory between API calls — only the context window.
common_mistakes: Believing the model 'understands' language the way humans do — it predicts the next plausible token, period.
Confusing the system prompt with the runner — the system prompt is *input*, not part of the runner.
Treating the model as having a 'memory' — every inference is stateless; state must be reconstructed each turn.
Assuming the runner differs between models — it's the same loop, with different weights plugged in.

topics.title: Tokens, Not Words: The Unit the Model Sees
concepts: Models do not see characters, words, or sentences; they see *tokens* — integers drawn from a vocabulary of 32K to 200K subword units (BPE, WordPiece, Unigram).
Tokenization is a lossy, learned compression of text. Many 'weird behaviors' of LLMs (bad spelling, can't reverse strings, bad at simple counting) trace back to it.
A 'token' is roughly 4 characters of English on average; non-Latin scripts tokenize into many more tokens per word.
Pricing of every commercial API is denominated in tokens, not words — making tokenization an economic and fairness concern.
Karpathy's 'Let's build the GPT Tokenizer' (2h13m) is the field's canonical demonstration of how tokenization determines behavior.
key_facts: BPE (Byte-Pair Encoding) greedily merges the most frequent adjacent pairs of bytes until the desired vocabulary size is reached.
GPT-2/3/4 use BPE via the `tiktoken` library; Llama uses SentencePiece (BPE/Unigram); BERT uses WordPiece.
Common failure: the model can spell 'strawberry' but cannot reliably count its 'r's — because the letters are packed into one or two tokens, not seen individually.
Tokenization is a security concern: adversaries can hide prompt-injection payloads inside subword splits that survive naive filters.
Llama 3 expanded vocabulary to 128K for multilingual efficiency; byte-level fallbacks cover anything unseen.
drills: Use the OpenAI `tiktoken` library to tokenize 5 sentences in English, then 5 in Japanese — observe the token-per-word ratio shift.
Try to ask GPT to reverse the string 'strawberry' — observe where and how it fails.
Reconstruct a chat template from raw token IDs without looking at the tokenizer — appreciate how much information is encoded in special tokens.
common_mistakes: Assuming '1 token ≈ 1 word' — it's ~¾ of a word for English, far worse for Chinese/Arabic/Russian.
Treating token limits as character limits — they are strictly token counts (a 4K limit is ~3K English words, far fewer non-Latin words).
Designing prompts that depend on letter-level fidelity (counting, reversing, capitalization) — these are token-level blind spots.
Forgetting that BPE on adversarial inputs can cause unexpected token boundaries that bypass filters (tokenization-induced injection).

topics.title: Embeddings as Coordinates in Learned Meaning-Space
concepts: An *embedding* is a learned vector (e.g., 1536 numbers) that places an item — a word, sentence, image, audio — as a point in a high-dimensional space.
Words with similar meanings end up near each other; analogies become arithmetic ('king' − 'man' + 'woman' ≈ 'queen' in early word vectors).
The space is *learned*, not given: the model discovers which directions correspond to meaning, sentiment, syntax, time period, even math-vs-prose.
Modern sentence embeddings (OpenAI v3, BGE-M3, Voyage) put semantically related text close together even across languages and modalities.
Embeddings are the bridge between symbolic AI and continuous math — every concept becomes a coordinate, every comparison becomes vector arithmetic.
key_facts: Word2Vec (Mikolov 2013) pioneered static word embeddings; the geometry of analogies emerged without anyone training for it.
GloVe (Pennington 2014) showed the same geometry could be obtained by factorizing the global word co-occurrence matrix.
Sentence-BERT (Reimers & Gurevych 2019) made BERT usable as a sentence encoder, birthing the modern retrieval stack.
Matryoshka Representation Learning (Kusupati 2022) allows truncating an embedding to fewer dimensions without destroying meaning — same model, many storage budgets.
Multimodal embeddings (CLIP, SigLIP, ImageBind) put text and images in the *same* space so a caption can retrieve an image and vice versa.
drills: Use the `sentence-transformers` library to embed 10 sentences, then use cosine similarity to find the nearest neighbor of each.
Sketch the vector arithmetic for 'king − man + woman' on paper using 2D toy embeddings — why does the geometry encode meaning?
Compare embeddings from two different models — observe they live in incompatible spaces.
common_mistakes: Conflating 'embedding' with 'word vector' — embeddings exist for tokens, sentences, images, audio, code, graphs, anything.
Assuming cosine similarity and dot product are interchangeable — they're only equal when vectors are length-normalized.
Treating embeddings as a private embedding space — many models leak training data through their embedding geometry.
Forgetting that similarity is *not* truth — a query can be very similar to a wrong doc and still get returned by RAG.

topics.title: Attention as a Soft, Differentiable Dictionary Lookup
concepts: Attention is a way for a sequence to look at itself: each position emits a *query*, *key*, and *value*; the query is matched against all keys, scores become weights, and the output is the weighted sum of values.
Mathematically: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QKᵀ / √d_k) V — a few lines that drive every modern LLM.
It is 'soft' (probabilistic over all positions, not a hard index) and 'differentiable' (gradients flow everywhere).
Multi-head attention runs several attentions in parallel, each learning a different relation (syntax, coreference, sentiment, etc.).
Attention is the entire reason transformers can model long sequences in parallel — no recurrence, no hidden-state bottleneck.
key_facts: Vaswani et al. ('Attention Is All You Need', 2017) made attention the *whole* architecture — no RNN underneath.
The √d_k factor prevents softmax saturation: without it, dot products scale with dimension and softmax gets stuck.
Decoder-only models (GPT) use *causal* masking: position i can only attend to positions ≤ i, not the future.
FlashAttention (Dao 2022) achieves the same math without ever materializing the N×N matrix in slow memory — IO-aware.
The cost is O(N²) in sequence length: the dominant reason long context is hard and FlashAttention and its successors matter.
drills: Compute scaled dot-product attention by hand for a 4-token sentence: write out Q, K, V, the QKᵀ matrix, and the softmax.
For 3 heads, what does each one 'look at'? Show with a tiny example that heads specialize.
Trace what happens to attention weights when you zero out the √d_k — predict the effect, then verify numerically.
common_mistakes: Treating attention as a 'lookup table' — it is *soft* (every position contributes, weighted by score) and *learned* (Q/K/V are learned projections).
Assuming larger context always helps — the 'lost in the middle' phenomenon means models ignore the middle of long contexts.
Forgetting that attention has no positional information — position must be injected separately (RoPE, ALiBi, learned).
Believing attention outputs are interpretable directly — they are; but BertViz shows each head is rarely a clean human concept.

topics.title: The Loss Is the Model: What Training Actually Optimizes
concepts: A neural network is a function with parameters; the *loss* says how wrong its predictions are on a batch of data; training pushes parameters to *minimize* the loss.
For LLMs the loss is next-token *cross-entropy*: how surprised the model was by the actual next token, averaged across all positions.
Mathematically: Loss = −(1/N) Σ log p_θ(x_t | x_<t) — same equation as the LLM training loop.
Whatever you put in the loss is what the model becomes. If you put next-token prediction in, you get next-token prediction.
The loss curve you see on a training plot is *the* report card. Perplexity = exp(loss); lower is better.
key_facts: Cross-entropy = entropy + KL divergence; minimizing cross-entropy = minimizing surprise = driving predictions toward truth.
Gradient descent: θ ← θ − η · ∇L(θ). This is the entire update rule of all neural networks.
Backprop is the chain rule applied recursively through the network — every gradient is a chain rule application.
Adam (Kingma & Ba 2014) maintains a per-parameter learning rate via running averages of the gradient's first and second moments.
Chinchilla (Hoffmann 2022): ~20 tokens of training data per parameter is compute-optimal for one training run.
drills: Compute the cross-entropy of a model that assigns probability 0.01 to the correct token at one position — what does the loss look like?
Sketch the gradient-descent update for f(x)=x² on paper, then explain why it's the equation for 'rolling a ball downhill.'
For one Adam step: given gradient g=10 and β1=β2=0.9, compute the bias-corrected moments and the parameter update.
common_mistakes: Believing loss ≈ accuracy — they correlate but a model can have low loss and still be confidently wrong on the cases that matter.
Thinking bigger models always have lower loss — they do on measured distributions but can be *more* confidently wrong off-distribution.
Confusing training loss with held-out loss — overfitting means the training loss is much lower than the test loss.
Optimizing only the loss — proxies can be 'gamed' by shortcuts (Goodhart's law; specification gaming).

topics.title: The Base Model Is Not the Assistant — Why Post-Training Matters
concepts: After pretraining on trillions of tokens, the base model is a *text-completer* — it can finish your sentence but won't answer questions, refuse bad requests, or hold a conversation.
Post-training (SFT + RLHF/DPO + sometimes RLAIF) transforms the base model into a helpful, harmless, honest *assistant*.
The same weights, with the right fine-tuning, become a chatbot, a coder, a translator, a reasoner — the alignment teaches a *behavior*, not new facts.
Sycophancy, over-refusal, and tone-shifts are RLHF artifacts — useful reminders that alignment is a behavioral overlay.
Modern frontier models spend most of their *post-training* effort on reasoning, tool use, and long-context behavior — not just chat.
key_facts: InstructGPT (Ouyang et al. 2022) defined the modern 3-step RLHF: SFT → reward model → PPO.
DPO (Rafailov et al. 2023) reformulated RLHF in closed form — no reward model, no RL — and is now the default post-training loss.
Constitutional AI (Anthropic, 2022) replaced human raters with the model itself, using a written 'constitution' of principles.
OpenAI's o1/o3 (2024) demonstrated that 'scaling test-time compute' (reasoning) rivals and beats 'scaling parameters' for hard problems.
Process Reward Models (PRM) reward every step of a CoT, not only the final answer — dramatically improving math/competition performance.
drills: Take an open base model (Llama-3.1-8B base) and the same model after instruct fine-tuning — ask each the same question, compare the responses.
Sketch the 3-step RLHF pipeline on a whiteboard: SFT → RM → PPO, with the KL penalty to the SFT model highlighted.
Try to fine-tune Llama-3 with DPO on a small preference dataset — observe how quickly behavior changes vs. how little knowledge changes.
common_mistakes: Assuming pretraining is where 'knowledge' lives — RLHF teaches behavior; base-model knowledge shows through RLHF.
Believing RLHF fully aligns the model — it shifts behavior on measured distributions; the internal objective is not guaranteed aligned.
Confusing alignment with safety — alignment is what the model tries to do; safety is the engineering discipline of ensuring it doesn't fail.
Treating reasoning models as 'cheating' — o1/o3 use more inference-time compute because for hard problems, it pays for itself.

modules.title: The Mathematical Foundations
modules.objective: Hold the exact mathematical primitives every ML practitioner uses daily: vectors, matrices, gradients, chain rule, Bayes, entropy/cross-entropy, KL divergence, gradient descent + Adam. These are not optional — every paper and every debugging session uses them.

topics.title: Vectors, Matrices, Tensors — The Shape Language of ML
concepts: A scalar is a single number; a vector is a 1-D list; a matrix is a 2-D grid; a tensor is the n-D generalization. Frameworks (PyTorch, TF) literally call these 'tensors' and index them by shape [d0, d1, ...].
Matrix multiplication C = AB has shape (m×n)·(n×p) → (m×p); inner dimensions must match; every entry C_ij is a dot product of row i of A and column j of B.
Ax = Σ_i x_i · A_:i — re-interpreting a matrix-vector product as a weighted sum of A's columns is the single most useful re-framing in ML.
The transpose mirrors across the diagonal: (Aᵀ)_ij = A_ji. Without transpose there is no dot product and no attention.
Broadcasting: in DL you can add a vector to every row of a matrix (C = A + b ⇒ C_ij = A_ij + b_j) — used in every batched forward pass.
key_facts: In math convention, vectors are columns (not rows); xᵀ is the row.
Matrix multiplication is not commutative (AB ≠ BA) but is associative and distributive.
Av = λv defines an eigenvector with eigenvalue λ — special directions that are only scaled, never rotated.
Eigenvectors of the covariance matrix give the axes of greatest variance — that's PCA.
A rank-k truncated SVD gives the *best* low-rank approximation (Eckart–Young theorem) — the math behind LoRA.
drills: For A = [[1,2],[3,4]] and x = [5,6]ᵀ, compute Ax by hand — confirm with numpy.
For two vectors in ℝ², decide if they are linearly independent, span ℝ², and form a basis — write down the reasoning.
Compute the SVD of a 2×2 matrix by hand using A = UΣVᵀ; verify that the singular values are non-negative and decreasing.
common_mistakes: Conflating matrix multiplication with element-wise (Hadamard) multiplication A⊙B — they are different operations.
Thinking 'more columns = higher dimension' — the dimension of the column space is capped by linear independence, not column count.
Assuming vectors and row vectors are interchangeable — they aren't; the shape notation is precise.
Forgetting that A and Aᵀ have different shapes — outer products and dot products depend on which is transposed.

topics.title: Dot Product, Norms, Cosine Similarity
concepts: The dot product x·y = Σ_i x_i y_i measures *alignment*; positive when vectors point the same way, zero when orthogonal, negative when opposite.
The L2 (Euclidean) norm ‖x‖ = √(Σx_i²) measures *length* — the as-the-crow-flies distance to the origin.
Cosine similarity cos(x, y) = (x·y) / (‖x‖·‖y‖) divides out length, leaving pure angle — range [−1, 1].
If vectors are L2-normalized (‖v‖ = 1), dot product *equals* cosine similarity *equals* 1 − ½‖x−y‖². Many libraries store normalized vectors and use the cheapest op (inner product).
Cosine similarity is the default for text embeddings (length-invariance matters) and image embeddings (lighting/scale invariance matters).
key_facts: L1 norm ‖x‖₁ = Σ|x_i| is the taxicab distance — preferred when you want sparsity.
L∞ norm ‖x‖∞ = max_i |x_i| — the worst-offender distance; used in adversarial-perturbation budgets (ℓ∞ attacks).
Frobenius norm ‖A‖_F = √(Σ_i,j A_ij²) — the L2 norm of a matrix, used in weight-decay regularization.
Two vectors can have zero covariance (zero correlation) and still be totally dependent (e.g., y = x²) — correlation captures only *linear* dependence.
Mutual information I(X;Y) = D_KL(P(X,Y) ‖ P(X)P(Y)) captures *all* dependence, not just linear.
drills: Compute ‖[3,4]‖₂, ‖[3,4]‖₁, ‖[3,4]‖∞ by hand, in different orders to make the differences click.
For two vectors [1,2,3] and [3,2,1], compute cosine similarity and dot product — see how cosine normalizes them to the same range.
Convince yourself that for two unit vectors, cosine and dot product give the *same* ranking of nearest neighbors.
common_mistakes: Believing 'cosine similarity = dot product' — only when vectors are length-normalized.
Forgetting that cosine scores are *not calibrated* across different queries — a 0.8 for one query doesn't mean more relevant than 0.75 for another.
Using L2 distance for asymmetric embedders — long documents will be 'far' from short queries even when semantically similar.
Tuning a similarity threshold on one metric and applying it to a different metric — the numbers mean different things.

topics.title: Derivatives and Gradients — Slopes, Partial Slopes, Directions
concepts: A derivative f'(x) = lim_(h→0) (f(x+h) − f(x))/h is the slope of the tangent at x — how much the output wiggles when you nudge the input.
A partial derivative ∂f/∂xᵢ treats all other inputs as constants and differentiates w.r.t. x_i.
The gradient ∇f = [∂f/∂x_1, ..., ∂f/∂x_n]ᵀ is a vector of partials — it points in the direction of *steepest ascent* (uphill); you *descend* by going in −∇f.
For neural nets, the gradient ∇_θ L(θ) tells each weight how to move to reduce the loss — this *is* the training step.
Jacobians generalize to vector-valued functions (m×n matrix of partials); Hessians are the Jacobian of the Jacobian (curvature).
key_facts: The derivative of x² is 2x; the derivative of eˣ is eˣ; the derivative of ln x is 1/x; the derivative of a constant is 0.
The product rule: (fg)' = f'g + fg'; the chain rule: (f∘g)' = f'(g) · g' — the chain rule *is* backpropagation.
Linearity of the gradient: ∇(αf + βg) = α∇f + β∇g, even for dependent variables.
In high dimensions, almost every critical point is a *saddle*, not a minimum — Dauphin et al. 2014 showed this is why deep nets train fine.
Newton's method uses H⁻¹ (the inverse Hessian) for curvature-aware steps; H is n×n with n=billions for LLMs, so we use first-order (SGD/Adam) instead.
drills: For f(x, y) = x² + 3y², compute ∇f at (1, 2) — verify the gradient points away from the origin.
Hand-compute the Jacobian of a 2-input / 2-output function — recognize that each layer of a neural net is a Jacobian times its inputs.
Show that the Hessian of f(x, y) = x² + 3y² is constant and positive-definite — hence f is strictly convex with one global minimum.
common_mistakes: Believing the gradient points toward the minimum — it points toward the *maximum* (uphill); descending requires subtracting it.
Conflating 'no gradient = minimum' — no gradient means stationary; could be a max, a saddle, or a plateau.
Forgetting the chain rule when plumbing gradients through a deep network — every 'no gradient' is a missed chain-rule application.
Thinking Newton methods are practical for deep learning — the Hessian is intractable; first-order is all we use.

topics.title: The Chain Rule — The Mathematics of Backpropagation
concepts: If y = f(g(x)), then dy/dx = (dy/dg)(dg/dx) — derivatives of compositions *multiply*.
A neural network is a long composition h = f_L ∘ ... ∘ f_2 ∘ f_1(x); backprop is the chain rule multiplied backward through this composition.
Local Jacobians at each node are multiplied along the path from loss to each parameter — that is reverse-mode autodiff.
The crucial efficiency identity: each node only needs its parents' gradients, not the whole graph re-evaluated ⇒ one forward + one backward gives *every* gradient.
Karpathy's micrograd (154 lines of Python) builds backprop from scratch — the canonical 'understand backprop by building it' artifact.
key_facts: Multivariable chain rule: if z = f(y₁,...,yₙ) and each yᵢ = gᵢ(x), then ∂z/∂x = Σᵢ (∂f/∂yᵢ)(∂yᵢ/∂x).
Backprop was independently derived by Werbos (1974), Parker (1985), Rumelhart/Hinton/Williams (Nature 1986).
Each modern framework (PyTorch, JAX, TF) implements backprop as reverse-mode autodiff triggered by `loss.backward()`.
Forward-mode autodiff (tangent-linear) is also possible; reverse-mode is cheaper because the network has millions of inputs and one loss.
Karpathy's 'Backprop Ninja' makemore lecture derives gradients through cross-entropy → linear → tanh → batchnorm → embeddings by hand — the gold-standard walk-through.
drills: Implement a `Value` class with `__add__`, `__mul__`, and `backward()`, then compute the gradient of f(x, y, z) = (x + y) * z at x=2, y=3, z=−4 by hand.
Trace the chain rule through f(x) = (x² + 1)² — show how df/dx = 4x(x² + 1) emerges.
In a 4-layer MLP, hand-derive the gradient of the loss w.r.t. the first-layer weights — name every chain-rule application.
common_mistakes: Believing backprop is a separate algorithm from the chain rule — they are mathematically identical; backprop is the chain rule organized to share subexpressions.
Thinking backprop is biologically implausible makes it wrong — biology isn't engineering; backprop is just efficient calculus.
Forgetting that PyTorch's autograd relies on the computation graph being intact — `torch.no_grad()`, in-place ops, and `.detach()` all break the chain.
Mistaking the *direction* of gradients — in backward, gradients flow from output to input, but each gradient is still a *local* chain-rule.

topics.title: Probability, Bayes' Rule, and Independence
concepts: Probability is the calculus of uncertainty: a number in [0, 1] (for masses) or non-negative and integrating to 1 (for densities) that quantifies belief.
Conditional probability P(y | x) = P(x, y) / P(x); independence means P(x, y) = P(x) P(y).
Bayes' rule: P(y | x) = P(x | y) P(y) / P(x). Posterior ∝ likelihood × prior. The single most useful re-arrangement in probabilistic ML.
Naive Bayes assumes features are conditionally independent given the class — 'naive' but historically powerful for spam filtering.
The chain rule of probability: P(x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ) = P(x₁) ∏ᵢ P(xᵢ | x₁, ..., xᵢ₋₁) — the factorization that *is* autoregressive language modeling.
key_facts: The base-rate fallacy: a '99% accurate' test for a 1%-prevalence disease still yields only ~50% posterior probability after a positive result.
Bernoulli (coin), Categorical (k-sided die), Gaussian (bell) are the workhorse distributions; softmax outputs categorical probabilities.
Most ML losses are expectations: L(θ) = E_(x,y)~data[loss(f_θ(x), y)], estimated by mini-batches.
Variance reduction (importance sampling, control variates) is the core difficulty of stable RLHF/PPO.
Mutual information I(X; Y) = H(X) + H(Y) − H(X, Y) = D_KL(P(X, Y) ‖ P(X)P(Y)) — info-theoretic measure of dependence.
drills: Solve the classic medical-test problem: 1% prevalence, 99% sensitivity, 99% specificity — compute P(disease | positive).
Given two fair dice rolls, compute the joint probability of 'sum ≥ 9' by enumeration and by the chain rule.
Show P(X, Y) = P(X) P(Y) holds iff X, Y are independent — give a counterexample where they are uncorrelated but dependent.
common_mistakes: Conflating P(A | B) with P(B | A) — the base-rate fallacy in disguise.
Believing 'uncorrelated = independent' — only one direction holds; covariance captures only *linear* dependence.
Forgetting that prior choice dominates the posterior in low-data regimes — MAP != MLE.
Treating a probability density as bounded above by 1 — densities can exceed 1; the constraint is non-negative and integrates to 1.

topics.title: Entropy and Cross-Entropy — The Loss Function of Modern AI
concepts: Entropy H(P) = −Σ P(x) log P(x) is the average surprise of a distribution — the optimal code length in bits (log₂) or nats (ln).
Cross-entropy H(P, Q) = −Σ P(x) log Q(x) is your average code length when the truth is P but you encode using Q.
The identity H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_KL(P ‖ Q) means minimizing cross-entropy is exactly minimizing the KL gap (data entropy is fixed).
For classification with one-hot labels, cross-entropy collapses to the negative log-likelihood: Loss = −log Q(correct class). *This is GPT's training loss.*
Perplexity = exp(cross-entropy) — the effective branching factor, 'how many equally-likely tokens the model was torn between'.
key_facts: Fair coin: H = 1 bit (0.693 nats). Uniform over k outcomes: H = log k. Loaded coin: H < uniform.
Bits = yes/no questions; nats = natural-log units; 1 nat = log₂ e ≈ 1.443 bits.
GPT/Llama/Claude pretraining all minimize average negative log-likelihood of the next token — see `F.cross_entropy(logits, targets)`.
Perplexity is the standard pretraining eval metric; a model's reported PPL is `exp(NLL per token)`.
Self-consistency (Wang et al. 2022) samples k CoTs and majority-votes on answers — a free accuracy bump when answers are discrete.
drills: For a binary classification with true class A and predicted P(A) = 0.7, compute the cross-entropy in bits and in nats.
Compute the entropy of a categorical distribution [0.5, 0.3, 0.2] — verify in nats and convert to bits.
For a 3-token sentence with per-token log-probs [-0.5, −1.2, −2.0], compute the total NLL and the perplexity.
common_mistakes: Confusing cross-entropy with KL divergence — they differ by H(P), which is the constant entropy of the data.
Thinking 'lower perplexity = always better' — PPL measures next-token prediction; reasoning benchmarks can drop while PPL stays flat (per the original Transformer paper's label-smoothing experiment).
Conflating entropy with thermodynamic entropy — they share the math but mean different things.
Treating cross-entropy as exotic — it IS the loss function of essentially every classifier and every LLM.

topics.title: KL Divergence — Measuring the Gap Between Distributions
concepts: D_KL(P ‖ Q) = Σ_x P(x) log (P(x) / Q(x)) — how many extra bits (or nats) you spend if you expect Q but the truth is P.
Always ≥ 0, and = 0 iff P = Q. Not symmetric: D_KL(P ‖ Q) ≠ D_KL(Q ‖ P).
Minimizing D_KL(Q ‖ P) makes Q 'cover' P (mean-seeking); minimizing D_KL(P ‖ Q) makes Q 'focus' on P's modes (mode-seeking). The direction matters.
Appears in VAE losses, knowledge distillation, RLHF KL penalties, DPO's reference term, and many others.
Mutual information = D_KL of joint from product-of-marginals — captures *all* dependence, unlike covariance.
key_facts: VAE loss = reconstruction + β · D_KL(q(z | x) ‖ 𝒩(0, I)) — the KL term shapes the latent to be a standard Gaussian.
Knowledge distillation (Hinton 2015) trains a student to match the teacher's soft labels by minimizing KL(teacher ‖ student).
RLHF adds D_KL(π_new ‖ π_ref) to prevent the policy from drifting off the SFT distribution and reward-hacking.
DPO's loss is derived from the optimal RLHF policy in closed form — it contains a KL term against a reference model.
Asymmetry example: P=[0.5, 0.5] vs Q=[0.9, 0.1]: D_KL(P ‖ Q) ≈ 0.511 nats; D_KL(Q ‖ P) ≈ 0.510 nats — close, but in general they differ wildly.
drills: For P = [0.5, 0.5] and Q = [0.9, 0.1], compute D_KL(P ‖ Q) and D_KL(Q ‖ P) — observe the asymmetry.
Sketch why D_KL(P ‖ P) = 0 always — use Gibbs' inequality.
Compute the per-token KL penalty in a PPO update given old and new policy log-probs for 5 tokens.
common_mistakes: Treating KL as a *distance* — it isn't symmetric and doesn't satisfy the triangle inequality; it's a *divergence*.
Forgetting that minimizing D_KL(Q ‖ P) vs D_KL(P ‖ Q) gives qualitatively different solutions (mean- vs mode-seeking).
Confusing KL with the Wasserstein/earth-mover distance — they capture different geometric properties.
In RLHF, setting the KL coefficient too low lets the policy reward-hack; too high and it can't move at all.

topics.title: Gradient Descent, SGD, and Adam — How Parameters Actually Move
concepts: Full-batch gradient descent: θ ← θ − η · ∇L(θ). The basic training step.
Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) uses a mini-batch gradient: an unbiased but noisy estimate; the noise even helps escape saddles.
Momentum: v ← βv + ∇L; θ ← θ − η · v. A heavy ball that smooths oscillations through narrow ravines.
Adam: keeps first moment (mean) and second raw moment (uncentered variance) of gradients, with bias correction, and uses them to compute *per-parameter* learning rates.
Adam's magic: a step size of approximately η regardless of the magnitude of the gradient — robust to gradient noise and scale.
key_facts: Adam's defaults: β₁=0.9, β₂=0.999, ε=1e-8, η=1e-3; the original Transformer paper used β₂=0.98 and a warmup-then-decay schedule.
Bias correction inflates m, v back to the correct scale at the start of training when they are biased toward 0.
AdamW fixes Adam's coupling between L2 regularization and the adaptive step size — the modern standard for LLM training.
SGD+momentum often generalizes *better* than Adam for vision tasks (Wilson et al. 2017); Adam is the default for LLMs.
Learning rate schedules — linear warmup, cosine decay, or step decay — often matter more than the optimizer choice itself.
drills: Minimize f(x) = x² by hand with η = 0.1 starting at x = 2 — walk 10 steps and observe the geometric decay.
For g₁=10, β₁=β₂=0.9, run one Adam step starting from m=v=0 — show why the step is normalized to ≈η.
Explain in one sentence why momentum helps but a too-large momentum overshoots narrow valleys.
common_mistakes: Cranking the learning rate 'to train faster' — past a threshold it diverges and weights NaN.
Confusing Adam (with L2 inside) and AdamW (decoupled weight decay) — most modern trainers use AdamW.
Believing 'Adam is always better' — for many vision tasks SGD+momentum generalizes better.
Forgetting to warm up — large lr at step 1 destabilizes early gradients; warmup is standard.

modules.title: How a Neural Network Learns — From Perceptron to Backprop
modules.objective: Be able to explain perceptron → MLP → backprop → regularization end-to-end, and to implement a small character-level language model in PyTorch from scratch.

topics.title: The Perceptron and the XOR Problem
concepts: Frank Rosenblatt's perceptron (1958) was the first machine that learned from examples: y = step(w·x + b), updated by w ← w + η(y − ŷ)x.
A single-layer perceptron is a linear classifier — it can only separate data that a hyperplane can split (linearly separable).
Minsky & Papert (1969) proved a single-layer perceptron cannot compute XOR — a famously simple non-linearly-separable function.
The fix was known conceptually (stacked layers), but lacked a clean training rule for hidden layers until backpropagation (1986).
The XOR crisis triggered the first 'AI winter' — funding dried up for ~15 years until enough compute and data returned.
key_facts: The Perceptron Convergence Theorem: if the data is linearly separable, the algorithm converges in finite steps.
Adding a hidden layer (an MLP) makes XOR learnable in seconds — non-linearity is load-bearing, not optional.
Modern 'perceptrons' are layer outputs: linear + nonlinearity + bias — the same shape, with backprop.
Stoy threshold ('step') is replaced by smooth activations (sigmoid, tanh, ReLU) so gradients flow.
Karpathy's micrograd builds a perceptron-equivalent from scratch in 100 lines of Python — the gate to true understanding.
drills: Train a perceptron by hand on OR; then try XOR; write down why XOR fails with a single line.
Show that adding a single hidden layer with two sigmoids makes XOR learnable.
Implement the perceptron learning rule in NumPy (no library) for AND, OR, NAND — verify each.
common_mistakes: Believing the single-layer perceptron's failure killed neural nets — it killed *one model*; the lesson pointed to deeper networks.
Conflating a perceptron with a modern neuron — modern 'neurons' are linear + nonlinearity + bias, trained by backprop.
Forgetting that depth + nonlinearity together are required — depth with linear activations collapses to one linear map.
Assuming the field of AI 'stopped' after 1969 — the connectionist thread kept going quietly through the 70s/80s.

topics.title: Multilayer Perceptrons (MLPs) and the Universal Approximation Story
concepts: An MLP is a stack of layers: h₁ = σ(W₁x + b₁); h₂ = σ(W₂h₁ + b₂); ...; ŷ = softmax(W_L h_{L-1}). Linear + nonlinear, repeated.
Width (units per layer) and depth (number of layers) trade off capacity; deeper is generally more expressive (residual connections make very deep nets trainable).
The Universal Approximation Theorem: a sufficiently wide 2-layer MLP can approximate any continuous function on a compact domain to arbitrary accuracy.
In practice we go *deep* rather than wide — deep models have compositional structure that wide shallow ones lack.
Karpathy's makemore Part 2 builds an MLP character-LM in ~150 lines: lookup, embed, hidden, output, train.
key_facts: Most modern networks are deep — 50–200 layers (GPT-3 has 96 layers; Llama-3-70B has 80; Claude is unpublished but comparable).
Skip / residual connections (He et al. 2015, ResNet) make 100-layer networks trainable by giving gradients a shortcut.
The Transformer position-wise FFN is literally an MLP: FFN(x) = max(0, xW₁ + b₁) W₂ + b₂ (with ReLU; modern variants use SwiGLU or GeLU).
Most MLP parameters live in the FFN; attention matrices in Transformers are smaller; capacity mostly lives in the feed-forward blocks.
GELU and SwiGLU have largely replaced ReLU in language models — smoother gradients near 0 and better empirical performance.
drills: Implement a 2-layer MLP from scratch (no PyTorch) for XOR — show it works when a perceptron cannot.
Train an MLP to fit a spiral — observe how hidden units tile space to carve the boundary.
For the makemore Part 2 MLP, run t-SNE on the hidden-layer activations at the end of training — interpret the clusters.
common_mistakes: Forgetting that without nonlinearity, depth is an illusion — the composition collapses to a single linear map.
Believing 'bigger is always better' — depth matters; over-parameterized shallow nets can't learn compositional structure.
Conflating parameter count with effective capacity — quantization, pruning, and low-rank approximation reduce the former without much loss in the latter.
Assuming an MLP can solve any problem given enough data — some tasks (long sequences, image grids) require architectural inductive biases (Transformers, CNNs).

topics.title: Backprop in Practice — What `loss.backward()` Actually Does
concepts: Modern deep learning frameworks implement reverse-mode autodiff; every tensor carries the computation graph that produced it.
Forward: every operation records its inputs and the local function on a tape. Backward: gradient is propagated backward via the chain rule, in the reverse order of operations.
The implementation only needs to write a forward and a backward for each op (matmul, add, relu, softmax, ...) — the framework composes them.
Memory: each tensor in the forward graph must be retained until backward; PyTorch frees memory with `torch.no_grad()` and explicitly with `del` / `torch.cuda.empty_cache()`.
checkpointing trades compute for memory by recomputing forward passes during backward; needed for very large models or activations.
key_facts: PyTorch's `loss.backward()` is the standard trigger; it walks the autograd graph once and fills `param.grad` for every parameter.
Mixed-precision training (BF16 forward / FP32 master weights) halves memory and ~doubles throughput on modern GPUs.
Gradient checkpointing reduces memory by ~O(√depth) at the cost of one extra forward pass — used in long-context models.
FSDP / ZeRO shards activations + gradients + optimizer states across GPUs — none of which would be possible without checkpointing and mixed precision.
JAX functionalizes the graph (`jax.grad`) — no in-place mutation, automatic vectorization via `vmap`, JIT via `pmap` for distributed training.
drills: In PyTorch, set `requires_grad=True` on a scalar, run a few ops, then print `tensor.grad` after `backward()` — see exactly which parameter received gradient.
Profile a 4-layer MLP training loop and measure time-forward vs time-backward — usually close to 2× in time and similar in memory.
Use `torch.utils.checkpoint.checkpoint` to enable activation checkpointing on a deep model — observe the memory drop and the compute cost.
common_mistakes: Forgetting `optimizer.zero_grad()` — gradients accumulate by default in PyTorch, silently doubling the effective step.
Modifying tensors in-place (`x.add_(1)`) when `requires_grad=True` — breaks the autograd graph.
Calling `backward()` inside a `torch.no_grad()` context — no gradients are stored.
Using `torch.no_grad()` for evaluation but forgetting it disables gradients you may need for analysis.

topics.title: Activation Functions and Why Nonlinearity Is Load-Bearing
concepts: Without activation functions, a stack of linear layers collapses to a single linear map — incapable of learning non-linear patterns (XOR, parity, image classes).
ReLU max(0, x): cheap, sparse, vanishing-gradient-resistant — the default for most vision models since 2012.
GELU x · Φ(x): smooth, slightly better than ReLU on Transformers; the default in BERT and many LLMs.
SwiGLU x · σ(W_gate x): three matrices instead of two; the default in Llama, Mistral, Qwen — best modern empirical quality.
Softmax σ(z)_i = e^{z_i} / Σ_j e^{z_j}: converts logits into a probability distribution over a vocabulary — the LLM output head.
key_facts: Sigmoid 1/(1+e^{-x}) saturates (gradient vanishes) for large positive/negative inputs — poisoned the early days of deep nets.
Tanh saturates too but is zero-centered; ReLU is both zero-half and unsaturated on the positive side — empirically much better.
Leaky ReLU / PReLU / GELU / SwiGLU all fix small failures of vanilla ReLU (the 'dying ReLU' problem).
The Transformer's `FFN(x) = max(0, xW₁ + b₁) W₂ + b₂` is a 2-layer MLP with ReLU; modern variants use SwiGLU and gain ~1–2 perplexity points.
Softmax's log-sum-exp trick: log Σ e^{x_i} = c + log Σ e^{x_i − c} for c = max(x_i) — every production softmax uses it for numerical stability.
drills: Train a tiny MLP on XOR with ReLU vs sigmoid vs no nonlinearity — observe that only ReLU converges cleanly.
Plot GELU, ReLU, SwiGLU side by side for x ∈ [−3, 3] — visually compare smoothness near 0.
Implement `softmax(x)` using the log-sum-exp trick — show why naive softmax overflows for large logits.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the activation — without it, depth adds no expressive power.
Choosing sigmoid as the final activation for a multi-class classifier — softmax is the right choice; sigmoid is for multi-label.
Believing 'ReLU is obsolete' — it's still competitive and far faster than the smooth alternatives; the difference is a few accuracy points.
Forgetting the numerical-stability trick for softmax — naive softmax overflows for large logits and silently returns NaN.

topics.title: Regularization, Normalization, Initialization — Keeping Networks Trainable
concepts: Regularization combats overfitting by penalizing complexity: weight decay (L2 = MAP with Gaussian prior), dropout (random neuron masking), data augmentation, early stopping.
Initialization sets the scale so activations don't explode/vanish: He init for ReLU, Xavier/Glorot for tanh/sigmoid.
Normalization rescales activations to have consistent statistics: BatchNorm (per-batch, training only), LayerNorm (per-sample,Transformer default), RMSNorm (simpler, Llama default).
Dropout (Hinton 2014) randomly zeros activations during training — a cheap Bayesian ensemble approximation.
AdamW decouples weight decay from the gradient adaptive step — it is the standard for modern LLM training.
key_facts: He init: Var(W) = 2 / fan_in for ReLU — preserves activation variance forward and gradient variance backward.
BatchNorm internalizes covariate shift — keeps activations zero-mean unit-variance per batch dimension; replaced by LayerNorm in Transformers because batch size varies.
LayerNorm is the default for Transformers (per-token, mean+var across feature dims); RMSNorm is its lighter cousin (variance only, no mean subtraction).
Dropout typically 0.1–0.3 in Transformers; less is used in modern LLMs (Llama-3 uses 0.0 in pretraining, SFT-only).
Gradient clipping (e.g., global-norm clip to 1.0) prevents exploding gradients in deep or RL-trained nets — standard in PPO.
drills: Train the same MLP with no init scheme vs He init — observe vanishing vs exploding losses.
Compare BatchNorm vs LayerNorm on a tiny Transformer — observe how each behaves when batch=1.
Add 30% dropout to a model and verify test loss drops while train loss rises — interpret the gap.
common_mistakes: Using the wrong initialization — random uniform breaks ReLU networks.
Forgetting to put a model in `eval()` mode for inference — BatchNorm uses running statistics instead of batch statistics.
Confusing weight decay with L2 regularization — in Adam they're different; AdamW is the modern fix.
Adding too much dropout — the network underfits; the empirical sweet spot is task-dependent.

topics.title: The Full Training Loop — Putting It All Together
concepts: A complete training loop: forward → loss → backward → clip grads → optimizer.step() → zero_grad → repeat.
Data is split into train / dev (validation) / test; train optimizes the loss, dev tunes hyperparameters, test reports final generalization.
Overfitting is detected when training loss drops but dev loss rises; remedies include regularization, more data, smaller model.
Hyperparameters to tune: learning rate, batch size, optimizer, dropout, weight decay, warmup, decay schedule.
Karpathy's full training-loop pipeline is in `nanoGPT/train.py` — ~300 lines, the gold standard for clarity.
key_facts: Train / dev / test must be disjoint and drawn from the same distribution; a held-out test set is the only fair judge of generalization.
Learning-rate finder: train at exponentially growing lr for a few hundred steps, plot loss vs lr, choose lr ~10x before the minimum.
Mixed precision (BF16) is essentially free on modern GPUs — speeds up training 1.5–2x with negligible loss in quality.
Cosine decay + linear warmup is the default LR schedule in Transformer pretraining.
Distributed training: data parallel (DDP), tensor parallel, pipeline parallel, FSDP — combine as '3D parallelism'.
drills: Write a from-scratch training loop (no PyTorch Lightning) for a tiny MLP — forward, loss, backward, step, log.
Split a small dataset 80/10/10 into train/dev/test; demonstrate overfitting by training too long.
Run a learning-rate finder on a real model — pick the recommended lr; verify train loss drops fast.
common_mistakes: Peeking at the test set during dev tuning — test becomes a second dev set, no longer fair.
Forgetting `optimizer.zero_grad()` — gradients accumulate silently across epochs.
Training at the wrong scale — a tiny model's conclusion may not transfer to a frontier model.
Treating training as a single fixed process — production training is an iterative experiment log, version-controlled.

modules.title: Vectors & Embeddings — Geometry as Meaning
modules.objective: Build deep intuition for word/sentence/multimodal embeddings; choose the right model for the job; understand similarity metrics, MTEB leaderboards, and the modern embedding zoo.

topics.title: The Distributional Hypothesis — 'You Shall Know a Word by the Company It Keeps'
concepts: Firth (1957): a word's meaning is captured by the words it appears near — distributional semantics.
Operationalized as 'embed words that appear in similar contexts with similar vectors'.
Pre-neural implementations: TF-IDF (sparse, high-dim) and LSA (SVD on the term-document matrix).
Holds remarkably well but breaks on antonyms ('hot'/'cold') and irony — the hypothesis is a regularity, not a theorem.
Bridges the symbolic world of language and the continuous world of vectors — every NLP model since inherits it.
key_facts: Pretrained GloVe was retrained on Dolma (220B tokens) in 2024 — 'A New Pair of GloVes' — the distributional hypothesis scales with data.
FastText encodes words as bags of character n-grams → captures morphology and handles out-of-vocabulary (OOV).
The hypothesis underlies Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT, GPT, every retrieval system built on text.
Antonymy is a known failure mode — 'hot/cold', 'up/down' share contexts but mean opposite. Mitigation: bilingual contrastive training.
The hypothesis predicts a *geometry of meaning* — and the geometry is observable in linear analogies (king−man+woman≈queen).
drills: Build a tiny TF-IDF vectorizer over 100 sentences; compute nearest neighbors for a query — interpret the geometry.
Train a Word2Vec model with `gensim` on a small corpus and examine the nearest neighbors of 'computer', 'happy', 'king'.
Hand-craft a tiny distributional-semantics co-occurrence matrix; factorize with SVD; project 2D and verify semantics.
common_mistakes: Confusing distributional similarity with semantic identity — they correlate but aren't the same.
Forgetting the hypothesis breaks on irony and sarcasm — production retrieval needs an additional check for intent.
Assuming the embedding dimensions are interpretable — they aren't; you can project them, but 'dimension 17' means nothing on its own.
Treating embeddings as ground truth — they inherit bias from their training corpus; the famous 'homemaker' bias is real.

topics.title: Word2Vec and GloVe — Static Word Vectors
concepts: Word2Vec (Mikolov et al. 2013): train a tiny shallow neural net to predict context (skip-gram) or a word from context (CBOW); throw away the network; keep the input weight matrix as the embedding.
Negative sampling reframes softmax-over-vocabulary into a set of cheap binary logistic regressions (positive pair vs k random negatives).
Subsampling frequent words ('the', 'a') accelerates training *and* improves rare-word vectors.
GloVe (Pennington et al. 2014): factorize the *global* word co-occurrence matrix so dot products ≈ log-counts; linear analogy geometry emerges from the *ratios* of co-occurrence.
Both produce *static* embeddings — the same vector regardless of context. 'Bank' riverside vs 'bank' finance are identical. This is the limitation that contextual models (BERT) solve.
key_facts: Word2Vec vectors are 100–300 dims, trained on Google News (~100B words) — a CPU can train this in hours.
The king−man+woman≈queen analogy was never explicitly trained; it emerges from the geometry of the loss.
GloVe is closer to LSA (matrix factorization) than to Word2Vec (local context); both end up at similar geometries because they capture similar statistics.
FastText (Bojanowski et al. 2017) extends Word2Vec with subword n-grams — better for rare words and morphologically rich languages (Finnish, Turkish).
The 'analogy arithmetic' is approximate and noisy — it became famous because it worked *surprisingly often*, not because it always does.
drills: Train a Word2Vec skip-gram with negative sampling on a small corpus — examine the nearest neighbors of 10 chosen words.
Compute 'king − man + woman' by hand from a pretrained GloVe — observe the nearest neighbor to the resulting vector.
Compare static (Word2Vec) vs contextual (BERT) embeddings for the sentence 'she sat by the bank' — which captures the polysemy?
common_mistakes: Believing Word2Vec 'understands' meaning — it encodes distributional co-occurrence, which correlates with meaning.
Forgetting order is ignored in static embeddings — 'dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' produce the same sentence vector after averaging.
Treating 'analogy arithmetic' as exact — it's an emergent regularity with wide error bars.
Using static embeddings for tasks that require polysemy (sentiment with 'cool', 'wicked') — use contextual embeddings (BERT) instead.

topics.title: Sentence and Document Embeddings (SBERT, OpenAI v3, BGE)
concepts: Sentence-BERT (Reimers & Gurevych 2019): two BERT towers with shared weights, fine-tuned on NLI for cosine-comparable pooled outputs.
Pooling choices: mean-pool > max-pool > CLS-pool for sentence similarity (per SBERT paper).
HuggingFace `sentence-transformers` and the `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` family are the workhorse — small, fast, decent quality.
OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small`/`large` (Jan 2024) are the hosted default; trained with Matryoshka Representation Learning so truncation to 256–1536 dims is graceful.
Cohere embed-v3, Voyage, BGE (BAAI), E5 (Microsoft), Instructor, gte, nomic — the modern zoo; MTEB is the canonical leaderboard.
key_facts: DPR (Karpukhin et al. 2020) showed dense retrieval beats BM25 by 9–19% on open-domain QA — killed the 'BM25 is unbeatable' myth.
Asymmetric embeddings need different prefixes/encoders for query vs document — Cohere `search_query` vs `search_document`; E5 `query:` vs `passage:`.
Multilingual: BGE-M3 (100+ languages, dense + sparse + multi-vector in one model, 8K context) is the open frontier.
Open models (BGE-M3, multilingual-E5, gte, Stella, nomic-embed) routinely beat closed APIs on MTEB — 'must pay' era is over.
Matryoshka (Kusupati 2022): embeddings trained so leading dimensions are valid embeddings on their own — coarse→fine signal.
drills: Embed 100 sentences with `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` and find the nearest neighbors of 'How does X work?' queries — evaluate qualitatively.
Compare embeddings from `all-MiniLM` (symmetric) vs `bge-m3` (asymmetric) on the same query/document pair — observe the length-mismatch effect.
Truncate an OpenAI v3 embedding to 256 dims vs a non-MRL embedding (e.g., ada-002) at 256 dims — compare MTEB scores.
common_mistakes: Using a symmetric embedder (e.g., MiniLM) for query→long-doc retrieval — the length mismatch hurts; use asymmetric.
Treating cosine scores as calibrated — they aren't across queries; rank (top-k) is more reliable than score thresholds.
Mixing embeddings from different models — `ada-002` and `text-embedding-3-small` live in incompatible spaces.
Forgetting to L2-normalize — many systems assume normalized vectors and use dot product = cosine.

topics.title: Multimodal Embeddings — CLIP, SigLIP, ImageBind
concepts: CLIP (Radford et al. 2021): an image encoder and a text encoder trained with contrastive loss over 400M (image, text) pairs — match captions to images.
The result is a *joint* space — you can do zero-shot ImageNet classification by embedding the image and comparing to text prompts of each class.
The 'label = text prompt' trick (with prompt ensembling) turned CLIP into a universal classifier and the visual-language glue of Stable Diffusion.
SigLIP (Google 2023): replace CLIP's softmax contrastive loss with a per-pair sigmoid loss — doesn't need a global softmax, scales batch to 1M.
ImageBind (Meta 2023): align 6 modalities (image, text, audio, depth, thermal, IMU) to *image* as anchor — emergent cross-modal retrieval without explicit text-audio pairs.
key_facts: CLIP was trained on 400M pairs scraped from the internet (WIT) — no human labels.
Zero-shot CLIP matches a supervised ResNet-50 on ImageNet (76.2% top-1) without seeing any ImageNet training images.
CLIP is *not* a captioner — it scores pairs; it has no generative decoder.
SigLIP + Locked-image Tuning (SigLiT) achieves 84.5% ImageNet zero-shot in 2 days on 4 TPUv4 chips.
Stable Diffusion's text encoder was originally CLIP's text tower; modern versions (SD3) use larger LLMs.
drills: Use `open_clip` to embed a photograph and a text caption — verify cosine similarity is highest for the matching pair.
Build a zero-shot image classifier with CLIP over CIFAR-10 — beat ~80% without training on CIFAR images.
Compare CLIP vs SigLIP cosine scores on a small held-out set — verify SigLIP's batch-scaling behavior.
common_mistakes: Believing CLIP 'understands' images — it scores similarity, not composition. It famously fails at counting and spatial relations.
Confusing CLIP with DALL-E — CLIP has no generative decoder; it's a dual-encoder retriever.
Assuming SigLIP and CLIP are interchangeable — SigLIP needs different downstream tuning but is faster and often more accurate.
Forgetting that CLIP's labels are prompts — prompt ensembling ('a photo of a {label}') typically beats a bare label.

topics.title: Similarity Metrics — Cosine, L2, Dot Product
concepts: Cosine similarity: cos(θ) = (a·b) / (‖a‖·‖b‖). Range [−1, 1] (often [0,1]). Only cares about angle, not magnitude.
Euclidean (L2) distance: ‖a − b‖₂. Range [0, ∞). Cares about position in space (direction + magnitude).
Dot product: a·b. Range (−∞, ∞). Cares about angle AND magnitude.
If vectors are L2-normalized, all three rankings agree: cosine == dot product == 1 − ½‖a − b‖².
Cosine dominates text embeddings (length-invariance), L2 matters for normalized vectors equivalently, raw dot product rewards long embeddings.
key_facts: OpenAI v3 / BGE / E5 / SBERT are trained with cosine (or contrastive) objectives — they expect cosine use.
Many libraries expect pre-normalized vectors: FAISS `IndexFlatIP` (inner product) on normalized vectors ≡ cosine.
Score thresholds aren't comparable across queries — a 0.8 for query A doesn't mean 'more relevant' than 0.75 for query B.
For sparse embeddings like BM25, the similarity is a length-normalized BM25 score — directly comparable within a query, less so across.
Dot product rankings can differ from cosine if magnitudes vary — long, frequently-occurring docs may 'win' by magnitude rather than meaning.
drills: Embed two vectors with `sentence-transformers`; compute cosine, L2, and dot product by hand — verify the normalized equivalence.
Add a long irrelevant document to a retrieval corpus and observe whether dot product or cosine is fooled more.
Tune a top-k cutoff for cosine scores across many queries — observe the variance to see why absolute thresholds are untrustworthy.
common_mistakes: Treating cosine and dot product as interchangeable without normalizing.
Using absolute score thresholds ('cosine > 0.7 means relevant') — these aren't calibrated across queries.
Using L2 on un-normalized vectors — magnitude dominates and 'similar' becomes 'same length' rather than 'same direction'.
Picking a similarity metric without checking what the embedding model was trained with — most text models expect cosine.

topics.title: The Modern Embedding Zoo — MTEB, Matryoshka, Asymmetric vs Symmetric
concepts: MTEB (Muennighoff et al. 2023) is the standard benchmark: 8 tasks × 58 datasets × 112 languages — the leaderboard everyone uses to pick an embedder.
Matryoshka Representation Learning (Kusupati 2022) trains embeddings so leading dimensions are valid on their own — OpenAI's text-embedding-3 supports a `dimensions` parameter for truncation.
Symmetric vs asymmetric: when query and doc are similar style, use a symmetric embedder; when they differ, use an asymmetric (DPR-style) one with separate query/doc prefixes.
Multilingual embedders: BGE-M3 (100+ langs, 8K context, dense+sparse+multi-vector), multilingual-E5, LaBSE — all open, free, strong.
Domain-specific embedders: Voyage (general), E5 (academic search), Instructor (task-instructable embeddings) — pick by use case.
key_facts: Closed APIs (OpenAI v3, Voyage) often top MTEB but cost money; open models (BGE-M3, gte, nomic) are competitive or better.
Matryoshka truncation saves storage cost without retraining — OpenAI v3-large at 256 dims still beats ada-002 at 1536 dims on MTEB.
Asymmetric prefixes change the embedding geometry — `query: X` vs `passage: X` encode differently so similarity is meaningful.
BGE-M3's 'M3' = Multi-linguality + Multi-functionality (dense/multi-vector/sparse) + Multi-granularity (8K context) — one open model replaces several.
The right embedder depends on (MTEB score on your task) + (multilingual coverage) + (context length) + (cost) + (symmetric vs asymmetric fit).
drills: Run an MTEB benchmark subset on three different embedders (e.g., BGE-M3 vs OpenAI v3 vs gte) — interpret the differences.
Embed the same query as a short question and as a hypothetical answer (HyDE-style) — observe the difference with symmetric vs asymmetric embedders.
Truncate an OpenAI v3 embedding to 256 vs 1536 dims and re-run a retrieval task — observe the Matryoshka graceful degradation.
common_mistakes: Picking 'the highest MTEB' model without reading which MTEB tasks — retrieval, classification, STS, clustering measure different things.
Using a symmetric model for query→long-doc retrieval — the length mismatch silently degrades recall.
Migrating embedders without re-indexing the entire corpus — embeddings live in incompatible spaces.
Forgetting that MTEB is a *proxy* — the gold test is your own held-out retrieval eval.

modules.title: Vector Databases & Retrieval — Finding Needles in Stacks of Embeddings
modules.objective: Choose the right ANN index for a workload, diagnose retrieval quality issues, and understand why modern RAG is a multi-stage engineering pipeline rather than a single embedding call.

topics.title: Why ANN — The Curse of Exact kNN at Scale
concepts: Exact kNN over n vectors of dimension d costs O(n·d) per query — fine at 10⁵, infeasible at 10⁹.
Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) trades a little recall for logarithmic-ish scaling — same precision budget per QPS.
Modern vector DBs cluster into two families: graph-based (HNSW, the production default) and quantization-based (IVF+PQ, FAISS heritage).
ANNS benchmark (ann-benchmarks.com) compares QPS-at-recall across ScaNN, HNSW (hnswlib), Annoy, FAISS, DiskANN.
Always measure the recall-latency-memory trade-off on your data — vendor benchmarks rarely match your production workload.
key_facts: HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World, Malkov & Yashunin 2016) is graph-based and the default in modern stores.
ScaNN (Google) leads on anisotropic quantization — biases quant error away from the query direction for higher recall-at-QPS.
DiskANN (Microsoft, 2019) is disk-resident; serves billions of vectors on a single machine cheaply.
Annoy (Spotify) is a forest of random-projection trees — read-only, memory-mapped, perfect for static datasets.
FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) is the reference GPU library; the entire ecosystem benchmarks against it.
drills: Sketch the HNSW search algorithm: start at the top layer, greedily walk toward query, descend, repeat.
Compare brute-force kNN on 100K vs 10M vectors — measure latency; understand why GPUs alone can't save you.
Trade off `ef_search` in HNSW: at low values you get high QPS but lose recall; at high values you invert the trade.
common_mistakes: Assuming 'more vectors are slow' is the only constraint — memory bandwidth usually is, especially for large d.
Benchmarking ANN on irrelevant data — every index has workload-dependent sweet spots.
Skipping recall measurement — fast search that returns wrong documents is worse than slow correct search.
Storing full vectors in RAM when DiskANN-on-SSD would suffice — wasteful for static corpora.

topics.title: HNSW — The Production Default
concepts: HNSW = layered proximity graph shaped like a skip list; each vector is randomly assigned to layers with exponentially-decaying probability.
Search enters at the top (sparse layer), greedily walks toward the query, descends through denser layers, refines.
Three hyperparameters: M (graph degree, ~16), ef_construction (build beam, ~200), ef_search (query beam — the recall/latency knob).
Logarithmic complexity: O(log n) per query for well-built HNSWs.
Used by Chroma, Weaviate, Qdrant, Pinecone serverless, Milvus, LanceDB, and pgvector's `hnsw` index — the de facto default.
key_facts: HNSW cannot easily be incrementally rebalanced — full rebuilds may be needed for major schema changes.
Pre-filter (filter then ANN) is more accurate than post-filter (ANN then filter), but slower.
HNSW is RAM-bound — every vector is stored explicitly plus a graph of ~M edges on average per layer.
Memory: for 1M 1536-dim vectors at M=16, expect ~3–5 GB RAM.
DiskANN (Microsoft) is the disk-resident sibling for ~billion-scale single-machine deployments.
drills: Use `hnswlib` to build an index of 1M synthetic vectors; measure QPS vs recall as you vary `ef_search` from 10 to 500.
Compare storage and QPS between `hnsw` and `ivfflat` in pgvector on the same dataset.
Insert a batch of 10K new vectors into an existing HNSW — measure the recall drop; rebuild and re-measure.
common_mistakes: Using brute force at 100K vectors — GPU is fine; HNSW is also fine; savings are negligible until 1M+.
Setting `ef_construction` too low — bad graph quality hurts QPS and recall.
Forgetting that HNSW is in-process — multi-process safety is up to you (read-only mmap is fine, writes are not).
Choosing HNSW when ANN quality matters more than memory — IVF+PQ with re-scoring beats HNSW at billion scale.

topics.title: IVF + PQ — FAISS Family, Billion-Scale on RAM
concepts: IVF (Inverted File Index): k-means partitions the space into Voronoi cells; query → find nearest nprobe centroids → search only those lists.
PQ (Product Quantization): split d-dim vector into m sub-vectors, quantize each to 256 centroids (8 bits) → ~m bytes per vector (vs 4·d bytes uncompressed).
IVFPQ: partition + compress. Hundreds of millions of vectors fit in RAM.
FAISS (Johnson, Douze, Jégou 2017) is the reference GPU library; competitive against custom systems at 1B vectors on 4 GPUs.
Trade-off: PQ is *lossy* — combine with re-ranking (exact distance on full vectors) at top-k for precision.
key_facts: PQ at m=48 compresses a 1536-dim float32 vector (6144 bytes) to ~48 bytes — 100× compression.
Training PQ takes time and a calibration set — train on a sample of your data, not random Gaussian.
Re-scoring is cheap: keep raw vectors for the top-100 candidates from compressed search and rank by exact distance.
GPU FAISS uses fused kernels for batched queries — single-machine billion-scale search in seconds.
IVF+PQ+HNSW hybrids exist — coarse partition via IVF, fine search via HNSW per cell.
drills: Train an IVF index with FAISS on 1M vectors; benchmark recall vs nprobe.
Add PQ to the IVF index; measure storage drop and recall drop; add re-ranking and observe recovery.
Compare latency of exact kNN (numpy) vs FAISS GPU vs FAISS CPU at 1M vs 10M vectors.
common_mistakes: Believing PQ is lossless — it's lossy by design; always benchmark recall.
Using PQ without re-ranking — the precision drop is rarely acceptable in production.
Training PQ on random data — quantizers must learn your distribution.
Choosing IVF for very small corpora — overhead dominates; use brute force or HNSW.

topics.title: Hybrid Search — BM25 + Dense + RRF
concepts: BM25 (Robertson & Walker ~1995) is the matured TF-IDF — term-frequency saturation + document-length normalization; remains a strong lexical baseline.
Dense embeddings catch *semantic* matches; BM25 catches *lexical* matches (exact IDs, error codes, named entities).
Fuse their rankings with Reciprocal Rank Fusion: score(d) = Σ 1/(k + rank_i(d)), k≈60 — no calibration needed; ranks only.
Convex combination of normalized scores is an alternative fusion but requires calibration.
Anthropic's Contextual Retrieval (Sept 2024): contextualized BM25 + contextualized embeddings — combined cuts retrieval failures from 5.7% to 2.9% (–49%), or 1.9% with reranking (–67%).
key_facts: DPR (Karpukhin et al. 2020) is the landmark paper: dense retrieval beat BM25 by 9–19% top-20 accuracy on open-domain QA, ending the 'BM25 is unbeatable' myth.
RRF is the de-facto fusion because it tolerates uncalibrated scores — only ranks matter.
SPLADE (Formal et al.) learned sparse representations that combine BM25's lexicality with neural training — best-of-both in one encoder.
Hybrid is table stakes — pure dense retrieval is considered naive in 2024+.
Cohere, Pinecone, Qdrant, Weaviate, Vespa, Elasticsearch all ship hybrid search with RRF.
drills: Implement BM25 from scratch on a small corpus — observe its strengths on exact-match queries.
Compare pure dense vs pure BM25 vs hybrid on a held-out retrieval eval — quantify the gap.
Use RRF to fuse dense and BM25 rankings — vary k (constant) and observe sensitivity.
common_mistakes: Pure dense retrieval on ID/code-heavy corpora — lexical signals are missed.
Convex-combining scores without calibration — RRF is robust precisely because it doesn't need it.
Mixing BM25 with dense embedding scores as if comparable — they're not; rank-based fusion is mandatory.
Skipping hybrid because dense looks decent in evals — the gap shows up the moment a query contains an ID or code.

topics.title: Common RAG Failure Modes — Lost-in-the-Middle, Chunk-Out-of-Context, Hallucinated IDs
concepts: Lost in the middle (Liu et al. 2023): LLMs exhibit U-shaped recall across long prompts — best at the start/end, worst in the middle.
Chunk-out-of-context: a retrieved sentence is correct in meaning but missing the disambiguating context ('revenue grew 3%' — whose, when?).
Hallucinated IDs/numbers: the model fabricates statute numbers, SKUs, error codes — plausible-looking but invented.
Bad citations: the model asserts a fact with no source, or cites the wrong chunk.
Stale knowledge: vector store not refreshed; the 'right' answer has changed.
Retrieval misses: query phrasing diverges from doc phrasing — fixed by HyDE, multi-query, hybrid search.
key_facts: Anthropic (Sept 2024): Contextual Retrieval (prepend LLM-generated context to each chunk) cuts retrieval failures 49%; with reranking 67%.
Late chunking (Jina 2024): embed the whole long document first, then chunk the embeddings — preserves document context in the vector.
Lost-in-the-middle fix: put the most relevant chunk at the start (or end) of the retrieved context — never in the middle.
MMR (Maximum Marginal Relevance) diversifies retrieval to avoid redundant near-duplicates in the context.
Self-RAG (Asai et al. 2023) trains the model to emit reflection tokens that decide *whether* to retrieve and whether generation is supported.
drills: Build a RAG pipeline; stuff 20 retrieved chunks into the context; observe how the model ignores or uses chunks at different positions.
Write a 'gold chunk' at position 1 vs position 10 in a 12-chunk prompt and measure answer correctness.
Build a Contextual Retrieval stub — generate a doc summary and prepend it to each chunk — observe retrieval recall improvement.
common_mistakes: Believing 'more chunks = better' — over-retrieval distracts the model; rerank + cap top-k.
Forgetting to put the most relevant chunk first — lost-in-the-middle ruins the answer even with the right doc.
Trusting the model's citations — verify them post-hoc; models will confidently cite nonexistent sources.
Skipping an eval suite — every RAG ship should have a held-out query→answer set with faithfulness, recall, answer-relevance scores.

modules.title: The Transformer Architecture — The Heart of Modern AI
modules.objective: Derive the Transformer from first principles; explain every component mathematically; understand why it became the dominant architecture and what its limits are.

topics.title: From RNNs to Attention — Why the Transformer Won
concepts: RNNs process sequences one token at a time — h_t = f(W·x_t + U·h_{t-1}); gradients either vanish or explode over long sequences (LSTM/GRU mitigate, not solve).
The bottleneck: sequential compute prohibits parallelization across time — GPUs sit idle; information must squeeze through one fixed-size hidden state.
Bahdanau attention (2014): decoder looks back at *every* encoder state via learned alignment weights — solves the bottleneck by being fully differentiable.
Luong attention (2015): dot-product scoring instead of a learned little net — simpler and faster; ancestor of the Transformer's formula.
The Transformer (Vaswani 2017): make attention the *primary* mechanism, drop the recurrence entirely — parallel, scalable, long-range.
key_facts: Vanilla RNN cannot learn dependencies >100 tokens; LSTM extends this to ~hundreds with the gated cell state.
Bahdanau's 'additive' attention: score(s, h) = vᵀ · tanh(W₁·s + W₂·h) — a tiny feed-forward net.
Luong's 'multiplicative' attention: dot, general, concat variants — fewer FLOPs, often comparable accuracy.
The Transformer paper showed that 6 layers of encoder + 6 layers of decoder, fully self-attention, surpassed LSTM+attention on translation.
Universal Transformers (Dehghani et al. 2019) showed transformers can be Turing-complete with the right depth/recurrence.
drills: Train a small LSTM and a small Transformer on the same sequence task — compare training time per epoch and final loss.
Implement Bahdanau attention for a tiny seq2seq — visualize alignment weights after training.
Sketch the gradient flow through an RNN for 500 steps and a Transformer — see the constant depth of the latter.
common_mistakes: Believing LSTMs 'solved' long dependencies — they mitigate vanishing gradients, not the parallelization bottleneck.
Forgetting that seq2seq with attention was the immediate predecessor to the Transformer — not invented in 2017.
Assuming Transformers removed all sequential structure — they removed it within training; autoregressive inference is still sequential.
Treating 'attention' as one technique — it is a family (additive, multiplicative, self, cross, sparse, linear).

topics.title: Scaled Dot-Product Attention — The Equation
concepts: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QKᵀ / √d_k) V — for each query, score every key by scaled dot product, softmax to weights, weighted sum of values.
Q, K, V are linear projections of the input: Q = X·W_Q, K = X·W_K, V = X·W_V — the only learned projections in a Transformer block.
QKᵀ is an (N × N) matrix of pairwise similarities — every position against every position.
Row-wise softmax gives a probability distribution per query; multiply by V for the output.
The √d_k factor prevents softmax saturation: dot products scale with dimension and would push softmax into a saturated peak without it.
key_facts: Q, K, V are *learned* — different projection matrices let the model use different 'views' of the same input.
The cost is O(N²·d) — quadratic in sequence length, the central scaling problem.
Vaswani footnote 4: if q, k components ~ N(0, 1), then q·k has variance d_k, hence the √d_k scaling.
Without scaling, dot products get huge for large d_k, softmax becomes one-hot, gradients vanish.
A 'differentiable dictionary lookup' is the canonical description — query asks, key answers, value is what gets returned.
drills: Compute attention by hand for 4 tokens of dimension d_k = 3 — write out QKᵀ, scale, softmax, multiply by V.
Compare attention with and without √d_k scaling on d_k = 64 — observe softmax saturation.
Implement attention from scratch in NumPy; verify against PyTorch's `F.scaled_dot_product_attention`.
common_mistakes: Confusing Q, K, V — they all come from the same input X but through different learned projections.
Forgetting the √d_k scaling — leads to immediate saturation in any model with d_k > ~16.
Believing attention is sparse by default — without masking, every position attends to every other.
Confusing attention outputs with similarity matrix — softmax of QKᵀ is the similarity; the output is its weighted sum of values.

topics.title: Multi-Head Attention & The Full Transformer Block
concepts: Multi-head: run h parallel attentions on different learned projections, concatenate outputs, final linear projection with W_O.
Total compute per head matches single-head attention when d_head = d_model / h — multi-head is essentially 'width for the attention path.'
Empirically heads specialize: some track the previous token, some attend to delimiters, some to syntactic roles (Clark et al. 'What does BERT Look At?').
A Transformer layer = (attention → add+norm → FFN → add+norm); residual connections let deep stacks train.
The FFN is two matmuls with a nonlinearity — it's where most of the model's *parameters* live (the FFN is ~2/3 of total).
LayerNorm (or RMSNorm) stabilizes activations; pre-norm is more stable for deep networks than post-norm.
key_facts: The original Transformer used h = 8 heads, d_model = 512, d_k = d_v = 64.
FFN dimension d_ff is typically 4 × d_model; modern GLU variants use ~2.67 × d_model × 2 for params.
Pre-norm (`x + MHA(LN(x))`) is the default in modern LMs — better gradient flow than the original post-norm.
Weight matrices in a Transformer: W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O, W_1, W_2 (per layer); summed up they account for almost all the parameters.
Most of a Transformer's 'knowledge' is in the FFN weights — attention is mostly *routing*.
drills: Implement multi-head attention from scratch — verify heads can specialize by training on a tiny synthetic task.
Inspect the weight histograms of attention vs FFN layers — see where the magnitude lives.
Run pre-norm and post-norm variants of a 24-layer Transformer — compare training stability and final loss.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the residual connections — removing them breaks deep stacks.
Using post-norm on very deep networks — gradients vanish; pre-norm is the modern default.
Conflating 'attention heads' with 'different concepts' — most heads are polysemantic; BertViz shows real specialization but it's rarely clean.
Believing the FFN is just an afterthought — it is where most of the model's capacity/knowledge lives.

topics.title: Positional Encodings — Injecting Order into Permutation-Invariant Attention
concepts: Attention is permutation-invariant by default — it has no notion of order; position must be injected separately.
Sinusoidal (Vaswani 2017): fixed sinusoids of varying frequency — no learned params, generalizes past training length.
Learned absolute (BERT, GPT-2): a learned embedding table indexed by position — fails to extrapolate.
RoPE — Rotary Position Embedding (Su et al. 2021): rotates the Q and K vectors by an angle proportional to position. Final attention scores depend only on relative position.
ALiBi — Attention with Linear Biases (Press et al. 2022): adds a linear penalty based on distance — enables length extrapolation without retraining.
Modern LLMs (Llama, Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek, Gemma) use RoPE with extension tricks (NTK-aware scaling, YaRN) for long context.
key_facts: RoPE's key property: ⟨q_m, k_n⟩ depends only on (m − n) — beautiful and the reason it generalizes.
ALiBi was developed to train short and test long; MPT, BloombergGPT adopted it.
Llama 3 uses RoPE with θ_base extended to 500K to enable 128K context.
Positional encoding choice interacts with FlashAttention kernel choices — RoPE-friendly kernels (FlashAttention v2+) are needed for production.
Some architectures (NoPE — no positional encodings) claim transformers can learn position implicitly — a controversial research thread.
drills: Implement sinusoidal positional encodings and visualize the first 100 × 100 dims as a heatmap.
Try to extrapolate a learned absolute PE beyond training length — observe the failure.
Re-implement RoPE rotation and verify that attention scores shift with the relative distance between positions.
common_mistakes: Forgetting positional encodings entirely — the model becomes a bag-of-tokens model that can't distinguish 'dog bites man' from 'man bites dog.'
Choosing learned absolute PEs when you need length extrapolation — use RoPE or ALiBi.
Conflating 'absolute position' with RoPE's encoding — RoPE encodes absolute via rotation but makes attention *relative*.
Adding too small a position space to embed — out-of-distribution positions break the model.

topics.title: Causal Masks and Encoder vs Decoder vs Encoder-Decoder
concepts: Causal (masked) attention: set QKᵀ entries above the diagonal to −∞ before softmax — position i sees only positions ≤ i.
Encoder: bidirectional self-attention — every position sees every other (BERT, RoBERTa, DeBERTa).
Decoder: causal self-attention — autoreg; GPT, Llama, Claude, Gemini are all decoder-only.
Encoder-decoder: cross-attention between encoder and decoder — T5, BART, original Transformer for translation.
Decoder-only + massive scale subsumed most encoder/encoder-decoder use cases after GPT-3 (2020).
key_facts: All modern chat models (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, Llama) are decoder-only with causal masks — no encoder, no cross-attention.
BERT-style encoders remain the workhorse for *understanding* (classification, NER) and as *embedding backbones* for retrieval.
Training-time efficiency: causal attention allows parallel computation across positions via teacher forcing (compute all logits at once).
Encoder-only vs decoder-only cost: same FLOPs per layer; encoders can be smaller for the same quality.
Universal Models (UniLM, GLM, PaLM-2-S) train a single model with multiple attention masks.
drills: Construct a causal mask for a 5-token sequence — verify position 2 is masked from positions 3, 4.
Compare self-attention with and without causal mask on a small text generation task.
Use a decoder-only checkpoint vs an encoder-only checkpoint for an embedding task — observe the matching score.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the causal mask during inference — without it the model 'cheats' and sees the future.
Forgetting the causal mask during *training* of an autoregressive model — teacher forcing still applies, but the mask is required for correct loss.
Using an encoder-only model for generation — it has no causal mask; the output is meaningless for next-token prediction.
Believing the encoder-decoder is dead — it persists in T5-family translation and summarization.

topics.title: Flash Attention — IO-Aware Exact Attention
concepts: Standard attention materializes the N × N attention matrix in HBM (high-bandwidth memory) — memory bandwidth is the bottleneck, not FLOPs.
FlashAttention (Dao 2022) tiles the computation and uses online softmax to never write the full matrix to HBM — keeps it in fast SRAM.
Bitwise equivalent to standard attention (modulo FP non-associativity) — same math, 2–8× faster, 5–20× less memory.
