FlashAttention-2 (2023): better work partitioning, fewer non-matmul FLOPs — 2× over FA1.
FlashAttention-3 (2024): targets Hopper, FP8 + async copy+GEMM overlap — 1.5–2× over FA2 on H100.
PyTorch 2.0+ `F.scaled_dot_product_attention` dispatches to FlashAttention automatically.
key_facts: FlashAttention made the 100K–1M-token context era possible — attention memory drops from O(N²) to O(N).
Frontier models (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, Llama) train with FlashAttention or DeepSpeed's equivalent.
FA3 enables FP8 attention on Hopper (H100) — retains quality under careful scaling and is used in some frontier training runs.
Online softmax (Milakov & Gimelshein 2018) is the algorithmic core: softmax can be computed incrementally through rows.
Long-context attention now stacks FlashAttention with RoPE scaling (NTK/YaRN), GQA/MLA, and sparse patterns.
drills: Time standard attention vs FlashAttention on a 16K-token sequence on a real GPU — measure the speedup.
Profile memory usage of a 32K-token forward pass — observe the O(N) reduction.
Inspect the attention outputs with and without FlashAttention — confirm mathematical equivalence.
common_mistakes: Believing FlashAttention is an approximation — it is exact, modulo non-associativity of floating point.
Forgetting FlashAttention has tile-size trade-offs — very long sequences may need explicit configuration.
Assuming FlashAttention makes all attention fast — bad algorithm choices (e.g., insufficient parallelism) still hurt.
Not enabling it in custom implementations — naive attention is far slower than the same ops in `F.scaled_dot_product_attention`.

topics.title: GQA, MQA, MLA — KV-Cache Efficiency Tricks
concepts: KV cache: at inference, store the keys and values of all past tokens to avoid recomputation; the dominant memory cost of long-context generation.
MQA (Multi-Query Attention, Shazeer 2019): all query heads share one K head and one V head — massive KV reduction with some quality loss.
GQA (Grouped-Query Attention, Ainslie et al. 2023): interpolate — g KV-head groups shared across h query heads; quality between MHA and MQA.
MLA (Multi-head Latent Attention, DeepSeek-V2/V3): compress K, V into a single low-rank latent per token — even smaller than GQA.
Llama 2 70B, Llama 3, Mistral, Gemma use GQA; DeepSeek-V3 uses MLA — both define the frontier long-context economics.
key_facts: KV cache memory per token: MHA = 2·d_model bytes (FP16); MQA = 2·d_head bytes; GQA = 2·g·d_head bytes; MLA ≈ 2·d_latent bytes.
For Llama 3 8B, MHA = ~32K tokens per request at FP16; GQA-8 ≈ ~120K; the difference is whether you can serve 32K contexts at all.
GQA matches MHA closely on quality when g is small (≤8 in practice) — the standard Llama recipe.
MLA's compressed-latent design requires specialized kernels — DeepSpeed and vLLM have MLA kernels.
vLLM's PagedAttention and TensorRT-LLM's in-flight batching both exploit GQA/MLA for throughput.
drills: Measure KV cache memory per request for an MHA, MQA, GQA-4, GQA-8 model on the same hardware.
Benchmark tokens/sec for MHA vs GQA-8 at 32K context length on a small Llama-class model.
Read the MLA section of the DeepSeek-V2 paper — understand the latent compression trick.
common_mistakes: Thinking GQA changes the math — it only changes how many K/V heads exist.
Forgetting to budget KV cache memory in serving — out-of-memory crashes on long contexts.
Assuming MHA is always best — for long context, GQA/MLA is the only way the model fits in H100 GPU memory.
Confusing MLA's latent compression with quantization — they are different tricks that can be combined.

modules.title: Building LLMs — The Engineering from Tokenization to Inference
modules.objective: Know what every step in the modern LLM pipeline does, why it matters, and which knobs to turn in production — tokenization, pretraining, distributed training, inference, efficient finetuning, and compression.

topics.title: Tokenization — BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece
concepts: BPE (Byte-Pair Encoding, Sennrich 2016): start from bytes, iteratively merge the most frequent adjacent pair until vocab size reached.
WordPiece (Schuster & Nakajima 2012): merges chosen to maximize likelihood; BERT.
Unigram LM (Kudo 2018): start big, prune tokens to maximize likelihood; SentencePiece supports BPE/Unigram/character.
SentencePiece (Kudo 2018): treats input as raw bytes — language-agnostic, no pre-tokenization; T5, Llama, Mistral, Qwen.
Vocab sizes: ~32K (Llama 2), 128K (Llama 3 for multilingual), 256 (byte-level).
Tokenization determines *failure modes* of the model — spelling, counting, reversing, capitalization.
key_facts: GPT-2/3/4 use BPE via `tiktoken`; Llama 3 uses SentencePiece with 128K vocab; DeepSeek uses ~100K vocab.
Karpathy's `minbpe` builds a working BPE tokenizer in <500 lines of Python — canonical teaching artifact.
Subword tokenization handles OOV and morphology; one representation per string is meaningful.
Tokenization can hide prompt-injection payloads: BPE-merged tokens bypass naive string filters.
Costs and fairness: many non-Latin scripts pay 3–10× more tokens per word — a quiet AI-tax issue.
drills: Implement BPE from scratch on a small corpus — show the merge sequences and the resulting tokens.
Use `tiktoken` to encode 50 sentences in English and 50 in Chinese — compare token counts per word.
Tokenize a string designed to bypass a substring filter; show how BPE merges can defeat naive input validation.
common_mistakes: Believing tokenization 'doesn't matter much' — bad tokenization is the root of many LLM failure modes.
Using `tiktoken` without realizing GPT-4's tokenizer is the wrong choice for code/multilingual — Llama 3 may be better.
Designing prompts that rely on letter-level operations — counting, reversing, capitalization.
Treating tokenization as a solved problem — it isn't; new tokenizers (e.g., for code, multilingual) are an active research area.

topics.title: Pretraining — Next-Token Prediction, Scaling Laws, Chinchilla
concepts: Objective: minimize average negative log-likelihood of the next token: L = −(1/N) Σ log p_θ(x_t | x_<t).
Scale: GPT-3 trained on 300B tokens; Chinchilla on 1.4T (70B params); Llama 3 on **15T tokens** (405B params); frontier pushes to 15–30T+.
Chinchilla (Hoffmann et al. 2022): compute-optimal balance ~20 tokens per parameter — GPT-3 was undertrained.
Modern frontier labs *intentionally overtrain* small dense models (e.g., 14B on 5T tokens) for inference efficiency.
Data quality curation dominates: dedup (exact + MinHash + SemDeDup), quality filtering (FineWeb-Edu score ≥ 3), safety scrubbing, decontamination against benchmarks.
key_facts: Next-token prediction is a 'universal' pretext task — grammar, facts, reasoning, translation, code all emerge if you train long enough.
FineWeb-Edu (HuggingFace 2024): 1.3T tokens of high-quality educational content, distilled into a classifier; vastly outperforms raw FineWeb.
Dedup with SemDeDup (Abbas et al. 2023): semantic near-duplicate removal via embeddings → ~50% data reduction with quality gains.
Decontamination: ensure benchmarks like MMLU are not in training data — a real risk for leaderboard validity.
Sasha Rush's NanoGPT speedrun community trains GPT-2 in minutes on a few H100s — peak pretraining efficiency.
drills: Train a tiny GPT on Shakespeare (nanoGPT, ~3 minutes) — watch loss decline and tokens become more coherent.
Replicate the Chinchilla 'tokens-per-parameter' calculation for a 7B model targeting 7T training tokens.
Compute the data-cleaning pipeline: dedup, quality filter, PII scrub, decontaminate — measure token counts at each stage.
common_mistakes: Training a bigger model instead of more data — Chinchilla and overtraining research show the data matters more for fixed compute.
Skipping decontamination — results on contaminated benchmarks inflate.
Trusting raw crawl data — ModernBB, RedPajama, FineWeb are the open canvases; your curation is the moat.
Memorizing the training loss — that's not the user-visible metric; perplexity on held-out data is.

topics.title: Distributed Training — 3D Parallelism (Data, Tensor, Pipeline)
concepts: Data parallelism (DP): each GPU has a full copy of the model, processes different batches, gradients averaged across workers (DDP, FSDP).
Tensor parallelism (TP): split each matmul across GPUs (Megatron-style) — needs fast NVLink interconnect.
Pipeline parallelism (PP): split layers across GPUs, micro-batches fill the pipeline — suffers a 'bubble' from imbalance.
Sequence parallelism (SP): split the sequence dimension across GPUs — for long-context training.
ZeRO-3 = FSDP: shard parameters, gradients, optimizer states across DP ranks — near-linear memory scaling.
3D parallelism = DP × TP × PP — how frontier models train across thousands of GPUs.
key_facts: Modern training: Megatron + DeepSpeed (NVIDIA/NVIDIA-Microsoft lineage); FSDP (PyTorch native); Ray + Kubernetes for orchestration.
Mixed precision: BF16 forward/backward, FP32 master weights — ~2× throughput, negligible quality loss.
FP8 training (Hopper/Blackwell) — 2× over BF16 with careful scaling; FlashAttention-3 enables FP8 attention.
Gradient checkpointing reduces memory by recomputing forward during backward — ~O(√depth) savings at +1 forward cost.
Sequence packing (concatenating sequences to fill context) recovers wasted tokens — Llama-3 recipe.
drills: Profile a 4-GPU training run of a 1B model: measure throughput, memory, and gradient synchronization cost.
Implement basic gradient accumulation across two simulated GPUs — observe effective batch size.
Compare ZeRO stages 1, 2, 3 on memory at fixed model size — quantify the trade.
common_mistakes: Skipping FSDP for large models — out-of-memory is the constant threat; sharding is the answer.
Forgetting that TP requires fast interconnect (NVLink) — falls apart on slow Ethernet.
Using FP16 instead of BF16 — BF16 has FP32's exponent range; FP16 needs loss scaling.
Forgetting that gradient checkpointing costs compute — only enable when memory-bound.

topics.title: Inference — KV Cache, Batching, Quantization, Speculative Decoding
concepts: Autoregressive inference is sequential — each new token needs the KV of all past tokens; KV cache dominates memory and bandwidth.
vLLM (Kwon et al. 2023): PagedAttention — virtual-memory paging for the KV cache — 24× throughput vs naive serving.
Continuous batching (in-flight batching) — answer finished requests as soon as they finish; never idle a GPU.
Speculative decoding (Leviathan 2023, Chen 2023): small draft model writes k tokens; big model verifies in one pass.
INT8/INT4 quantization (GPTQ, AWQ, NF4): shrink model memory 2–4× with negligible quality loss; bitsandbytes's NF4 makes 70B fit on a MacBook.
Multi-GPU serving: tensor parallel across inference nodes; sometimes pipeline; FlashAttention-Decode for high-QPS long-context.
key_facts: KV cache memory: 2 · num_layers · num_kv_heads · head_dim · seq_len · bytes per value; doubles for BF16.
KV cache dominates H100 GPU memory for long-context serving — GQA/MLA is the dominant cost mitigation.
Speculative decoding speeds up autoregressive inference 2–3× with identical outputs — no quality loss.
Production serving frameworks: vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang, llama.cpp, LMDeploy — different trade-offs (throughput vs latency vs flexibility).
TensorRT-LLM uses fused kernels, INT4/INT8, in-flight batching — Frontier-quality latency at frontier-scale throughput.
drills: Use vLLM to serve a small Llama-class model; measure tokens/sec at 1, 16, 64 concurrent requests.
Compare naive KV cache (concatenating tensors) vs PagedAttention on memory waste.
Quantize a 7B model to 4-bit with bitsandbytes; verify quality with perplexity on a held-out sample.
common_mistakes: Using greedy batching — wastes GPU time; always use continuous batching.
Skipping KV cache paging — naive serving has 60–80% memory waste on the cache.
Quantizing without evaluating — quality loss is dataset-dependent; benchmark before shipping.
Forgetting that speculative decoding changes latency, not throughput — use it for interactive workloads.

topics.title: LoRA, QLoRA — Efficient Fine-Tuning at All Scales
concepts: LoRA (Hu et al. 2021): freeze the big weight matrix W; learn a low-rank update ΔW = B·A where A ∈ ℝ^{r×d}, B ∈ ℝ^{d×r}, r ≪ d.
Trainable params drop by ~10,000×; GPU memory ~3× lower; adapters can be hot-swapped for per-user variants.
The rank-r hypothesis: adaptation updates have low intrinsic rank; rank 8–64 captures nearly all the change.
QLoRA (Dettmers et al. 2023): 4-bit quantized base + LoRA adapter + paged optimizers + NF4 quantization. Fine-tune 65B on a single 48GB GPU.
DoRA (Liu et al. 2024): decomposes weights into magnitude + direction; outperforms LoRA at similar param count.
Production: PEFT library (Hugging Face) unifies LoRA/QLoRA/AdaLoRA/etc.; OpenAI custom GPTs, Cursor, Claude model customization all use LoRA internally.
key_facts: Empirical sweet spot: LoRA r = 16 to 64 on attention projections (Q, K, V, O); many users also add it on FFN.
Merging LoRA into the base weights at deployment time saves inference cost — no separate forward pass.
QLoRA's three innovations: NF4 (4-bit NormalFloat matched to Gaussian distribution of weights), double quantization (quantize the constants), paged optimizers (NVIDIA unified memory).
Multi-LoRA serving (S-LoRA, OpenLLM): host one base model + many adapters; choose per request.
PEFT vs full fine-tuning: PEFT for behavior/style and small data; full FT for deep knowledge injection or major capability changes.
drills: Fine-tune Llama-3-8B with LoRA (r=16) on a small dataset — note memory savings vs full FT.
Compare LoRA r=8, 32, 128 — observe quality saturates quickly.
Try QLoRA — fine-tune a 13B model on a single 24GB GPU and verify quality parity with LoRA.
common_mistakes: Using LoRA when you actually need full FT for knowledge injection — LoRA is for behavior/style.
Forgetting to merge LoRA back for serving — separate adapters cost extra forward time.
Using too small a rank — underfitting; too large — diminishing returns.
Believing PEFT matches full FT always — it doesn't for deep capability shifts.

topics.title: Quantization, Distillation, Pruning, Merging — The Compression Toolkit
concepts: Quantization: store weights in FP8/INT8/INT4 instead of BF16 — halves or quarters memory; minor quality loss.
PTQ (post-training quantization): GPTQ, AWQ, bitsandbytes NF4; no training, seconds–minutes per layer.
QAT (quantization-aware training): simulate quantization during training — recovers most quality loss but retraining cost.
Distillation (Hinton 2015): small student model trained to match teacher's logits (soft labels) — often beats a larger model trained from data alone.
Pruning: SparseGPT, Wanda (Sun, Liu 2023) — remove ~50% of weights with little quality loss; structured pruning removes entire heads/layers.
Model merging: SLERP, TIES, DARE (Yadav et al. 2023) — average or interpolate weights of multiple fine-tunes; 'model soup' can match SFT.
key_facts: GPTQ (Frantar 2022): one-shot INT4 quantization for billion-parameter models in ~hours.
AWQ (Lin 2023): activation-aware weight quantization — preserves outliers, runs INT4 with minimal accuracy loss.
Phi-3-mini: distilled to ~3.8B params, beating 7B+ models — distillation genuinely helps.
SLERP weight interpolation: average two fine-tunes smoothly across a weight-loss landscape; sometimes better than either parent.
Mixture-of-Depths (MoD, Raposo 2024): per-token compute routing — each token uses as many layers as it needs; cuts inference cost ~50%.
drills: Quantize a 7B model to INT4 with bitsandbytes; check perplexity on a held-out sample vs BF16.
Distill a 7B teacher into a 1B student using logit-matching loss — observe the gap.
Use Wanda pruning on a model — inspect sparsity patterns and final perplexity.
common_mistakes: Quantizing without benchmarking — quality loss is benchmark- and dataset-dependent.
Pretending INT4 has 'no loss' — some tasks degrade >2pp; measure before shipping.
Believing distillation is free — the student's architecture and capacity still matter.
Forgetting to merge LoRA before pruning — both work on the same weights, order matters.

modules.title: Post-Training — SFT, RLHF, DPO, RLAIF
modules.objective: Know the post-training pipeline end-to-end: when to use SFT vs preference optimization vs test-time reasoning; understand the trade-offs between RLHF, DPO, and the constitutional family.

topics.title: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) & Instruction Tuning
concepts: SFT = supervised training on (instruction → ideal response) pairs — the first step after pretraining to turn a text-completer into an assistant.
SFT teaches *style, format, and steerability* — pretraining supplies the knowledge.
FLAN (Wei 2021) showed that cross-task instruction tuning generalizes to held-out tasks (zero-shot transfer).
InstructGPT (Ouyang 2022) staged it: SFT → reward model → RLHF. Modern pipelines add reasoning data, persona data, and tool-use data to SFT.
Open SFT datasets: OpenAssistant (OASST), Dolly 15K, OpenHermes, Magpie (synthetic), UltraChat, HelpSteer.
key_facts: RLHF-tuned (chat) models need *far fewer* shots than raw base models — often zero-shot beats few-shot.
SFT alone changes behavior enormously but RLHF/DPO is what makes responses reliably *preferred*.
Quality, diversity, and coverage of failure modes matter more than the volume of SFT data.
SFT can introduce *mode collapse* and *sycophancy* — the same patterns RLHF then sometimes has to fix.
Magpie (Xu et al. 2024): synthetic SFT data from a real chat LLM with no human labels — bootstraps large datasets cheaply.
drills: Fine-tune Llama-3-8B base with LoRA on a small OpenAssistant subset — measure quality shifts with held-out prompts.
Compare SFT-only vs SFT+DPO — observe style vs preference deltas.
Inspect Magpie-generated SFT data — observe quality without any human labels.
common_mistakes: Believing SFT 'teaches facts' — it teaches format and behavior; knowledge comes from pretraining.
Treating 'more data = better SFT' — quality and diversity dominate.
Using base-model SFT for chat — needs strict format (ChatML, Llama-3 instruct template) for tokenizer round-trips.
Skipping held-out evaluation — silent regressions are easy to introduce in SFT.

topics.title: RLHF — The Original 3-Step Pipeline (SFT → RM → PPO)
concepts: Christiano et al. 2017: first RLHF (Deep RL from Human Preferences) — Atari/MuJoCo demonstrations.
Stiennon et al. 2020: applied RLHF to summarization.
InstructGPT (Ouyang 2022): the canonical 3-step pipeline that became ChatGPT.
Step 1: SFT on demonstrations. Step 2: train a reward model (RM) on human preference labels (A vs B → Bradley-Terry). Step 3: PPO with KL penalty to the SFT model.
The KL penalty prevents reward hacking — policy drifts but stays close to the reference.
key_facts: RLHF teaches *preference* — what humans find more helpful/harmless — not facts.
Reward models can be reward-hacked: a policy that produces confident-sounding but wrong answers can score highly against a weak RM.
PPO is the standard RL optimizer; it's unstable for language models — hyperparameters matter a lot.
KL is computed against the SFT model (π_SFT) — KL weight too low → reward hacking; too high → no movement.
Open datasets: HH-RLHF (Anthropic), UltraFeedback (GPT-4-generated), HelpSteer (NVIDIA).
drills: Implement a Bradley-Terry reward model on a small pair dataset — observe the loss curve.
Sketch the 3-step pipeline on a whiteboard with the KL divergence highlighted on the arrow from RM to PPO.
Analyze an UltraFeedback dataset — note the criteria, the rationale, and the label variance.
common_mistakes: Treating RLHF as 'teaching the model facts' — it steers behavior.
Ignoring reward-model quality — a buggy RM corrupts the policy.
Skipping the KL penalty — the policy reward-hacks its way to high RM scores.
Running PPO without a robust KL implementation — divergence halts training unpredictably.

topics.title: DPO & the Preference-Optimization Family (IPO, KTO, SimPO, ORPO)
concepts: DPO (Rafailov et al. 2023): a closed-form RLHF — the optimal policy has a ratio of chosen-to-rejected log-probs, regularized by a reference model. Skip the reward model and PPO entirely.
Loss directly widens the gap between chosen and rejected, with the reference model preventing drift.
One stage, no RL instability, no PPO clipping — and matches/beats RLHF in many settings.
IPO, KTO, SimPO, ORPO are variations addressing DPO's edge cases (overfitting, unary labels, length bias, reference-free).
The 2024 default for open post-training is DPO/SimPO/ORPO; KTO when only unary labels exist.
key_facts: DPO is mathematically equivalent to RLHF in closed form — a theoretical, not heuristic, result.
KTO (Ethayarajh 2024): Kahneman-Tversky Optimization — uses thumbs-up/thumbs-down only, modeled on prospect theory.
SimPO (Meng 2024): reference-free, length-normalized reward — simpler and often better than DPO.
ORPO (Hong 2024): monolithic — folds SFT and preference into one loss with no reference model; cheap.
DPO is back in vogue for *preference*; RL is back for *reasoning* (verifiable rewards in math/code).
drills: Implement DPO loss from the equation — verify against a reference implementation on a toy preference dataset.
Train a model with SimPO and compare to DPO on a held-out preference benchmark.
Try KTO on a binary-feedback dataset — observe the symmetry of good/bad signal.
common_mistakes: Treating DPO as a different 'philosophy' from RLHF — they are mathematically the same.
Using DPO with overly narrow preference data — overfits fast and becomes brittle.
Skipping the reference model in DPO (use SimPO/ORPO if you want to drop it).
Believing DPO fixes alignment — it shifts behavior on measured distributions.

topics.title: Constitutional AI & RLAIF
concepts: Constitutional AI (Bai et al. 2022): replace *human* raters with an *AI rater* that critiques and revises against a written 'constitution' of principles.
Two phases: (1) supervised revision — model critiques own response against a principle, rewrites; (2) RLAIF — AI generates preference pairs, then RLHF or DPO on the pairs.
Decouples alignment quality from human-label volume; scales the *judgment*.
RLAIF (Lee et al. 2023) showed AI feedback can match human feedback on summarization.
Core to Claude's training pipeline — Anthropic's flagship alignment technique.
key_facts: The constitution is written by humans; the *judgments* are made by the model — humans are in the loop at design time, out at label time.
Constitutional AI doesn't *guarantee* safety — a constitution is only as good as its principles and the model's ability to apply them.
Runtime constitutions: append your own principles to a system prompt and have the model self-check outputs.
Self-rewarding LLMs (Yuan 2024): the model generates its own preference data and judges itself — iterative self-improvement loop.
Weak-to-strong (Burns 2023): can a weak supervisor elicit the full capability of a strong model? Partially yes — an analogy for human supervision of superhuman AI.
drills: Write a 5-principle 'constitution' for a customer-service chatbot; use Claude/GPT to revise a draft response against it.
Implement a simple RLAIF loop — generate pairs with a critic LLM, train with DPO, iterate.
Inspect public Claude and OpenAI system prompts — note how constitution-style principles are encoded.
common_mistakes: Assuming Constitutional AI is fully automatic — humans still write the constitution.
Putting vague principles in the constitution ('be helpful') — needs specificity ('never share customer PII without explicit consent').
Believing self-critique removes all bias — the model inherits its own blind spots.
Confusing AI-rater feedback with AI-judged self-improvement — neither solves the scarcity-of-ground-truth problem.

topics.title: Process Reward Models & Test-Time Reasoning
concepts: Outcome Reward Model (ORM): rewards the final answer; standard RLHF for short, objective tasks.
Process Reward Model (PRM): rewards *every step* of a CoT — better for multi-step math/reasoning.
Lightman et al. 2023 (PRM800K): step-level labeling massively improves math performance over outcome supervision.
Test-time compute: search over reasoning paths at inference, scored by a PRM (MCTS, beam search, best-of-N).
OpenAI o1/o3 (2024), DeepSeek-R1 (Jan 2025), Claude extended thinking, Gemini 2.5 Thinking — all use PRM-driven reasoning at inference.
key_facts: Reasoning models spend tens of thousands of tokens per question thinking — o1 reportedly improves Codeforces 11th → 89th percentile.
RL with verifiable rewards (math, code) is more stable than RLHF on preference data — R1 was trained with GRPO.
Distilling reasoning into smaller models works — R1-Distill (1.5B–70B) often matches much larger non-reasoning models on math.
Verifiers (PRMs) are *predictors* of step correctness — they generalize but are imperfect.
Cost/latency tax: reasoning is expensive; route based on problem difficulty (model cascading).
drills: Train a small PRM on a math dataset; use it to rerank multiple CoT outputs from a base model.
Compare ORM vs PRM final accuracy on a small math benchmark — observe PRM's advantage.
Use a model cascade: try cheap reasoning first, escalate to o1-style model only on difficult prompts.
common_mistakes: Believing reasoning is 'magic' — it's search + verification, both imperfect.
Forgetting the reasoning-trace can be unfaithful (Turpin 2023) — what the model 'says' it thought isn't what it did.
Using reasoning for trivial queries — wastes tokens and latency; route by difficulty.
Treating PRMs as perfect — they fail on adversarial or out-of-distribution steps.

topics.title: When to Fine-Tune vs Prompt vs RAG vs Tools
concepts: Matching intervention to gap: knowledge → RAG/tools; behavior/style → SFT/DPO; long-horizon reasoning → test-time compute; fresh data → tools.
Fine-tuning is for behavior, not facts — RAG solves hallucination, fine-tuning often *worsens* it.
Long-context vs RAG is a continuum — long-context dominates when the corpus fits and is stable; RAG when scale, freshness, cost, provenance matter.
Tools give the model capabilities it doesn't have (calculator, code interpreter, web search) — never bake volatile knowledge into weights.
Agents combine RAG + tools + memory + planning — for unstructured goals and long horizons.
key_facts: Fine-tuning fixes 'how the model says things', not 'what it knows.'
Use SFT/DPO to lock in style/format/persona; use RAG to ground knowledge; use tools for everything fresh.
Reasoning via test-time compute for hard multi-step problems; cheap models for everyday ones.
Continued pretraining for new domains/topics base model never saw; SFT for chat-format shifts.
When everything else fails, prompt-cache + long context — fits surprisingly often.
drills: Walk through a real product scenario — pick the right intervention for each gap (knowledge vs behavior vs reasoning).
Sketch a tool-use loop that falls back from primary doc to web to human handoff when each fails.
Build a decision tree: 'Should I fine-tune?' with the canonical answer ('No' until SFT is justified).
common_mistakes: Fine-tuning to 'fix hallucination' — usually worsens confident wrongness; use RAG.
Adding tools that the model can't reliably call — function-calling accuracy matters.
Treating long context as a RAG replacement — cost, lost-in-the-middle, and provenance all hurt.
Confusing continued pretraining with SFT — they target different gaps (knowledge vs behavior).

modules.title: Prompting, RAG & Agents — From Conversation to Capability
modules.objective: Build production-grade LLM applications: structured prompts that work, RAG that retrieves the right docs, agents that can plan and use tools, and tool-use standards that compose.

topics.title: Prompting Patterns — CoT, Self-Consistency, ToT, ReAct, Least-to-Most
concepts: Zero-shot: just ask. Few-shot (Brown 2020, GPT-3): demonstrations in the prompt — bias the distribution toward the shown pattern.
Chain-of-Thought (Wei 2022): 'Let's think step by step' — emergent at ~60B+ params; +GSM8K accuracy by tens of points.
Self-consistency (Wang 2022): sample k CoTs at high temp, majority vote — GSM8K +18% accuracy.
Tree-of-Thoughts (Yao 2023): branch, self-evaluate, backtrack — Game-of-24 from 4% to 74% with GPT-4.
ReAct (Yao 2022): interleave Thought → Action → Observation — agent loop skeleton.
Least-to-most (Zhou 2022): decompose hard problem into sequential sub-problems — generalizes to longer/harder than shown.
Self-Refine (Madaan 2023): model critiques its own draft, revises — ~20% gain across tasks without extra training.
Constitutional Decoding: model self-checks against a written set of principles at sampling time.
key_facts: CoT is *emergent* in capability — small models see no benefit or degradation.
CoT is also a debugging artifact — you can read where reasoning breaks.
Self-consistency works only for tasks with a discrete, extractable final answer.
ToT is expensive (many LLM calls per problem) and overkill when CoT suffices.
Anthropic guidance: be clear and direct; use XML tags; give a role; prefill the response; few-shot for non-obvious formats; chain-of-thought in `<thinking>` tags.
drills: Compare zero-shot vs few-shot vs CoT vs self-consistency on 10 hard math problems — quantify the gain.
Implement a ReAct loop (Thought/Action/Observation) on a tiny calculator tool — observe the agent learn.
Use Self-Refine on a draft essay — measure quality delta after one or two iterations.
common_mistakes: Forcing CoT on small or incapable models — can hurt accuracy.
Sampling at temperature 0 with self-consistency — paths don't diverge.
Using ToT when CoT already nails it — expensive without proportional gain.
Believing CoT 'makes the model think' — it produces more reasoning-like text; the causal link is contested.

topics.title: Structured Outputs & Function Calling
concepts: JSON mode (OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini): constrain decoding to valid JSON syntax — does NOT guarantee schema shape.
Structured Outputs (OpenAI, Aug 2024): strict JSON-schema adherence; `additionalProperties:false`, all fields required — guaranteed match.
Function/tool calling: the model emits a structured call to a tool with typed arguments; your code executes; the model consumes `tool_result` in the next turn.
Grammar-constrained sampling (OSS): constrain the token sampler to a formal grammar (JSON, regex, custom) — Outlines, Guidance, LMQL.
Parallel tool calls: model emits multiple tool calls in one turn; runtime executes concurrently.
tool_choice modes: auto / required / specific tool / none — control the routing.
key_facts: OpenAI introduced function calling June 2023 (gpt-3.5-turbo-0613 + gpt-4-0613); Anthropic tool use went GA Oct 2024.
Description fields in tool definitions are the *real prompt engineering* (ACI — Agent-Computer Interface).
Model does *not* execute anything — it proposes calls; your runtime executes.
Toolformer (Schick 2023): self-supervised tool-use learning before function-calling APIs — kept only calls that reduced next-token loss.
Anthropic Contextual Retrieval (Sept 2024) showed descriptions and tool schemas matter as much as code.
drills: Build a calculator tool with structured output — verify the model emits parseable arguments consistently.
Use Outlines to constrain generation to a regex — observe the trade-off between constraints and reasoning quality.
Simulate an agent with parallel tool calls — measure latency vs sequential.
common_mistakes: Conflating JSON mode with schema validation — JSON mode doesn't enforce your schema; use Structured Outputs.
Designing function-calling tools without good descriptions — models follow the descriptions, not the function bodies.
Retrying on parse errors instead of constraining decoding — pray-and-parse should not be your strategy.
Forgetting to sandbox code execution — model-generated code running on your box is a security boundary.

topics.title: RAG Engineering — Contextual Retrieval, Late Chunking, Reranking
concepts: Original RAG (Lewis 2020): seq2seq generator + dense Wikipedia index; RAG-Sequence (one passage per output) vs RAG-Token (different passage per token).
Contextual Retrieval (Anthropic, Sept 2024): prepend LLM-generated 50–100 token context summary to each chunk before embedding + BM25 indexing; prompt caching makes it ~$1.02/M tokens.
Late Chunking (Jina 2024): embed the whole document through a long-context embedder first, then chunk the embeddings — preserves global context.
Query rewriting: HyDE (generate hypothetical answer, embed that), multi-query (rephrase in parallel), step-back (rewrite abstractly).
Reranking: take top-100 from bi-encoder retrieval, cross-encode to top-5–20 with Cohere Rerank 3 / BGE-reranker / Jina Reranker.
MMR (Maximum Marginal Relevance): diversify the retrieved set to avoid feeding duplicates to the LLM.
key_facts: Anthropic's numbers: contextual embeddings cut failures 5.7%→3.7% (−35%); +BM25 → 2.9% (−49%); +rerank → 1.9% (−67%).
Top-k selection: Anthropic found top-20 beats top-5/10 (more chances for the right chunk).
Lost-in-the-middle (Liu 2023): put the most relevant chunk at the start or end of the prompt, not buried.
Hybrid (dense + BM25) is table stakes — pure dense is now considered naive.
RAGAS (Es 2023): faithfulness, answer relevance, context precision/recall — the LLM-judged eval suite for RAG.
drills: Build a naive RAG; then add Contextual Retrieval; then add reranking — measure retrieval recall at each stage.
Compare top-5 vs top-20 retrieval with reranking on a small eval set.
Use RAGAS to evaluate a RAG pipeline — inspect which metrics reveal which failure modes.
common_mistakes: Mixing dense and BM25 scores directly (without RRF) — they're not calibrated.
Skipping the retrieval eval — perception of quality and measured recall diverge quickly.
Believing 'bigger chunks' are safer — they dilute relevance; right size depends on corpus and chunking strategy.
Forgetting to version your embeddings — model upgrades silently invalidate the index.

topics.title: Advanced RAG — Self-RAG, CRAG, FLARE, GraphRAG
concepts: Self-RAG (Asai 2023): model emits reflection tokens ([Retrieve], [Relevant], [Supported]) that decide *when* to retrieve and whether the generation is grounded.
CRAG (Yan 2024): a lightweight retrieval evaluator scores chunks as correct/ambiguous/incorrect → falls back to web search when local retrieval is poor.
FLARE (Jiang 2023): iteratively generate; if a sentence has low-probability tokens, retrieve *for that sentence* — active retrieval.
GraphRAG (Edge 2024): LLM extracts an entity knowledge graph from the corpus; community-cluster summaries address global/summarization questions that flat RAG can't.
Modular retrieval pipelines (Modular RAG, Self-Ask, etc.) chain these patterns together.
Agentic RAG: the retrieval tool is one capability among many — the agent decides if/when/what to retrieve.
key_facts: Self-RAG beats ChatGPT on open QA / fact-verification; big citation-accuracy gains.
FLARE is ideal for long-form synthesis — just-in-time retrieval aligned with generation needs.
GraphRAG beats naive RAG on global questions for ~1M-token corpora (Microsoft paper, arXiv:2404.16130).
RAG-triad evaluation: context precision/recall (retrieval), faithfulness (use of context), answer relevance.
Agentic RAG trades predictable latency for flexibility — needs iteration caps and budgets.
drills: Implement a tiny Self-RAG-style loop — let the model decide when to call a `search` tool.
Build a CRAG-style fallback — score retrieval confidence and route to web search when below threshold.
Run GraphRAG on a small news corpus — query global questions like 'what are the main themes this quarter?'
common_mistakes: Applying GraphRAG to small corpora where simple RAG suffices — it's an expensive preprocessing pipeline.
Forgetting budgets in agentic RAG — agents can spin indefinitely without iteration caps.
Skipping retrieval evaluation — agentic RAG is harder to debug without it.
Treating reflection tokens as garbage collectors — they're the heart of self-RAG.

topics.title: MCP — The Universal Connector for AI Tools and Data
concepts: Model Context Protocol (Anthropic, Nov 25 2024) — open standard for connecting LLMs to data sources and tools.
Client-server architecture: MCP servers expose data/tools; MCP clients (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Claude Code) consume them.
Three primitives: Tools (model-invoked actions), Resources (app-exposed data), Prompts (reusable templates).
Transport: JSON-RPC over stdio (local) or Streamable HTTP (remote).
Solves the N×M integration problem — write the server once, every MCP-aware client works.
Launched with Google Drive, Slack, GitHub, Postgres, Puppeteer servers; early adopters Block, Apollo, Zed, Replit, Sourcegraph.
key_facts: MCP is a protocol, not an agent runtime — it standardizes how tools are described and called, not the loop.
MCP does NOT make tools safe — same prompt-injection risk applies (tool outputs can contain malicious instructions).
Claude Code is MCP-native; Cursor, OpenAI Assistants API, IDEs all adopt/consume MCP servers.
MCP is the leading candidate for a durable tool/data connector standard (analogous to USB-C for AI).
Best practice: tools should be scoped, capability-checked, and audited — MCP server codebases are now production-grade.
drills: Write an MCP server that exposes a simple document-search tool — consume it from Claude Desktop.
Run two MCP servers simultaneously (one local, one remote) — observe multi-server composition.
Inspect an MCP server's resource and tool definitions — note description quality and prompt engineering.
common_mistakes: Confusing MCP with an agent framework — MCP is a transport/protocol; the agent lives elsewhere.
Treating MCP-wrapped tools as 'trusted by default' — they're third-party code reading your context.
Building a custom protocol instead of using MCP — fights the emerging standard.
Not scoping tools — broad MCP servers can call broad APIs; gate by capability.

topics.title: Agent Loops, Memory, and Planning — ReAct, Reflexion, MemGPT
concepts: The agent loop: plan → act (tool) → observe → reflect → repeat until done or budget hit.
ReAct (Yao 2022): the foundational pattern — Thought/Action/Observation are interleaved.
Planning: Plan-and-Execute (decouple plan from execution), Tree-of-Thoughts, Least-to-Most, LLMCompiler (DAG-parallel).
Memory tiers: working (context window), episodic (past interactions), semantic (distilled facts); MemGPT/Letta (Packer 2023) treats context as RAM and storage as disk with the model paging.
Reflexion (Shinn 2023): agent writes a verbal self-reflection on failure, stores it in episodic memory, retries — 91% HumanEval pass@1.
Multi-agent: ChatDev (Qian 2024), MetaGPT, AutoGen, CrewAI, LangGraph — role-played specialized agents with debate/coordination.
key_facts: Anthropic's framing: workflows (predefined code paths, predictable) vs agents (dynamic control over how to accomplish).
Anthropic's advice: start with the simplest solution (a single LLM call + retrieval + in-context examples); add agentic complexity only when justified.
CodeAct (Wang 2024): make the *action* executable code (Python) — composability beats JSON tool calls (+20% accuracy).
Multi-agent systems have *N× cost* and coordination overhead — they help for separation of concerns and debate but rarely are worth it alone.
Production agent harnesses: LangGraph, DSPy, smolagents, Pydantic-AI, CrewAI, AutoGen — pick by architectural fit, not hype.
attack_techniques: AML.T0051 — LLM Prompt Injection (indirect via tool output)

attack_side.technique: Prompt-injection and tool-output laundering are the canonical agent attacks: malicious instructions hidden in retrieved docs, emails, web pages, or tool results are consumed by the agent and treated as control flow.
attack_steps: Step 1: Plant injected instructions in any data the agent will read — RAG corpus, email contents, search results, web pages, calendar attachments, file uploads.
Step 2: The injected text overrides or supplements the user's prompt; the agent may exfiltrate data, send unwanted emails, change settings, or delete files.
Step 3: Chain benign tools (search → summarize → translate → email) to launder a harmful request past individual filters.
Step 4: Exploit agent memory — poisoned memories persist across sessions and corrupt future turns.
tools: Crafted web pages (prompt injection in invisible HTML)
Tool-output injection (e.g., from a compromised API)
Memory poisoning via agent feedback loops

defense_side.what_to_detect: Defenders should monitor agent behavior: tool-call provenance, retrieval-source inspection, output validation failures, repeated/looping tool calls, attempted destructive actions, off-policy responses.
logs: LangSmith / Weave / Phoenix / OpenLLMetry traces — every tool call, retrieval, observation.
Retrieval-source audit trails (which documents were passed to the agent).
Budget/latency counters per session (detect infinite loops).
detection_rules: Sigma: tool call outside expected set / unusual arguments.
Sigma: retrieval result containing instruction-like phrasing (heuristic + ML).
Alert: agent attempted to write/call destructive actions (rm, git push, file overwrite, email send) without approval.
Anomaly: more than N tool calls per turn (rate cap).
controls: Dual-LLM pattern (Willison) — planner with tools + sandboxed reader for untrusted content.
CaMeL (Debenedetti 2025) — capability-based, untrusted data never becomes control flow.
Sandbox (container, no network, resource limits) for code execution.
Human-in-the-loop gating for destructive actions.
Prompt caching with explicit untrusted-tagged content.

topics.gap_analysis: Most deployed agents have no defense against indirect prompt injection and would silently comply with attacker instructions hidden in retrieved data. The dual-LLM/CaMeL patterns are well-documented but rarely implemented in production.
drills: Implement a ReAct loop in 50 lines — give it a calculator and a search tool; run on a tiny problem.
Build a MemGPT-style agent with hierarchical memory — observe the agent paging context in/out.
Run Reflexion on a tiny coding task — observe episode memory and self-critique.
common_mistakes: Building an agent for tasks a single LLM call could solve — added complexity for no gain.
Forgetting iteration caps — agents loop indefinitely without budgets.
Trusting tool outputs as instructions — the SQLi of AI; defend with isolation not instruction-tagging.
Conflating memory with context window — persistent memory is a separate, auditable surface.

topics.title: Coding & Computer-Use Agents — The Frontier Application
concepts: SWE-bench (Jimenez 2024): real GitHub issues → codebase edits across multiple files; leaderboard for coding agents.
SWE-agent (Princeton): the canonical research agent for SWE-bench; Agent-Computer Interface design matters as much as the model.
Tools: Aider (terminal pair-programmer), Claude Code (agentic CLI), OpenAI Codex CLI, Cursor, GitHub Copilot Workspace, Devin (Cognition).
Anthropic Computer Use (Oct 2024): Claude trained to see screenshots and emit mouse/keyboard actions (bash, computer, text-editor tools).
OpenAI Operator (Jan 2025): browser-operating CUA agent; Google Project Mariner, Apple Intelligence, Adept ACT-2 in the same family.
Coding agents are the first frontier because code is *verifiable* (tests pass/fail), iterable (test results as feedback), well-structured, and measurable.
key_facts: Anthropic identifies coding as the flagship agent domain because of verifiability and feedback density.
Multi-step coding agents now achieve >50% on SWE-bench Verified (Claude 3.5/3.7, GPT-4o, etc.).
Computer-use agents are early but capable — human-equivalent on many GUI tasks with safety caveats (confirmation before destructive actions).
OpenAI's o1/o3 and DeepSeek-R1 were first reasoning models — both markedly better at competitive programming than non-reasoning predecessors.
Effective coding-agent stacks: planner + editor + linter + test-runner + interpreter; agents that can verify their own work are 3–10× more productive.
attack_techniques: AML.T0051 — LLM Prompt Injection (repository README / commit message / issue text payloads)

attack_side.technique: Coding agents inherit the full attack surface of software supply chains: poisoned dependencies, malicious README payloads, exfiltration via `.env` reads, dependency-confusion, malicious tool calls in the planning step, and silent workflow drift.
attack_steps: Step 1: Plant injected instructions in any file the agent reads — README, issue text, commit message, code comments, CI config.
Step 2: Agent reads the file and incorporates the instruction; may exfiltrate secrets, modify code, or trigger backdoors.
Step 3: Hijack the agent's tool calls — make it read `.env`, fetch environment variables, run an exfiltration command.
Step 4: Exploit agent's iteration loop — keep the agent running until cumulative effect is catastrophic.

defense_side.what_to_detect: Defenders should detect unusual file access (`.env`, `~/.aws/credentials`, private keys), unexpected network calls, execution of obfuscated shell, repeated destructive actions without human approval.
logs: Coding-agent execution traces (Claude Code, Aider, Cursor audit logs).
File-read audit (every file the agent touched).
Repo-wide git diff after each agent session.
detection_rules: Sigma: agent reads `.env`, key files, credentials files.
Sigma: agent calls curl/wget with user-data or environment vars in args.
Sigma: agent runs obfuscated/base64 commands.
Alert: agent attempts git push without explicit approval.
Anomaly: agent performs >N file edits per session (loop detection).
tools: GitGuardian / TruffleHog for secret scanning post-session
Semgrep for static analysis of agent-generated code
Anthropic Constitutional Classifiers on coding-agent outputs
controls: Sandbox every coding-agent tool execution (no network except approved domains).
Human approval for git push, file delete, dependency install.
Read `.env` / credentials blocked by default.
Run tests after every agent edit; do not commit if tests fail.
Isolated worktree per agent session; reset between sessions.

topics.gap_analysis: Most enterprise coding-agent deployments lack adequate secret scanning and audit logging for agent sessions — agents have read the same `.env` files they were hired to refactor.
drills: Use Aider on a small repo — observe how it decomposes tasks into edits + tests.
Try Claude Code on a non-trivial refactor — observe its planning loop and tool use.
Set up a coding agent in a sandboxed environment without network access — observe its limits.
common_mistakes: Letting coding agents run without sandboxing — they can exfiltrate or wipe your environment.
Skipping human-in-the-loop on destructive actions — `git push` and `rm -rf` need gating.
Treating the agent's edits as production-ready without review — even strong models make subtle mistakes.
Forgetting to scope tools — broad file/network access expands the blast radius dramatically.

modules.title: AI Safety Foundations & Threat Landscape
modules.objective: Be able to argue about AI safety fluently — the threats, the regulations, the responsible-AI principles, and the canonical attack/defense taxonomies (OWASP LLM Top 10, MITRE ATLAS).

topics.title: The Three Horizons of AI Harm — Accidents Today, Adversaries Today, Agentic Alignment Tomorrow
concepts: Horizon 1 — Accidents: the model does something harmful because it's *wrong* (hallucination, bias, privacy leak). The bulk of production incidents.
Horizon 2 — Adversaries: humans weaponize the model or break its guardrails (prompt injection, jailbreaks, extraction).
Horizon 3 — Agentic alignment: as models become more capable and agentic, will they robustly do what we intend even when we're not watching?
Most production incidents are Horizon 1 + 2; most existential-risk debate is Horizon 3 — conflating them is the #1 teaching error.
The capability-vs-alignment axis: a super-capable misaligned system is the worst case; capability without alignment is the field's worst-case scenario.
key_facts: Most AI harm today: bias, hallucination, privacy leakage, scams, non-consensual imagery — not Skynet.
Alignment vs capability: capability is raw competence; alignment is the degree to which objectives match human intent.
Outer alignment = the *specified* objective matches the *intended* objective; inner alignment = the *learned* internal objective matches the *specified*.
Specification gaming / reward hacking: optimize the proxy, not the goal — 'hold a mop in front of a sensor so it reads clean.'
AI safety principles (Microsoft): Fairness, Reliability & Safety, Privacy & Security, Inclusiveness, Transparency, Accountability — mnemonic 'FRP-ITA.'
drills: For 10 real-world AI incidents (Tay, Amazon recruiter, Mata v. Avianca, Samsung code-leak), classify them into Horizon 1/2/3.
Walk through a concrete spec-gaming example (e.g., a boat-racing RL agent spinning in circles for points).
Sketch the FRP-ITA principles as a checklist for a real product — apply each to a candidate LLM feature.
common_mistakes: Conflating 'AI safety' with only existential risk — ignores 95% of the field's actual work.
Treating safety and security as the same team's problem — different threat models, different tooling.
Believing RLHF 'solves' alignment — shifts behavior on measured distributions; internal objective not guaranteed.
Equating more capability with less safety — often the opposite (more capability can hide bias more confidently).

topics.title: Responsible AI Principles & Operationalizing Them
concepts: Microsoft's six principles (Fairness, Reliability/Safety, Privacy/Security, Inclusiveness, Transparency, Accountability) — the canonical industry set.
NIST AI RMF 1.0 (Jan 2023): four core functions — Govern → Map → Measure → Manage — voluntary but actionable.
Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights (OSTP, Oct 2022): five principles (Safe Systems, Discrimination Protections, Data Privacy, Notice, Human Alternatives) — aspirational.
Model cards (Mitchell 2019): standardized documentation of intended use, evaluation results, ethical considerations.
Responsible Scaling Policies (Anthropic) and Preparedness Frameworks (OpenAI) tie capability thresholds to required safety evals.
Operationalizing is the gap: principles to behavior, behavior to measurement, measurement to action.
key_facts: Datasheets for Datasets (Gebru 2021): the model card for data — required context for any production model.
Impact assessments (DPIA, FRIA under EU AI Act) require concrete risk analysis, not platitudes.
Most 'responsible AI' frameworks have a Govern function — without organizational buy-in the rest is decoration.
EU AI Act: tiered obligations; 'systemic-risk' GPAI (>10^25 FLOPs) requires model evals + adversarial testing + incident reporting.
Internal red teams are the operational backbone — external policy without internal testing is theater.
drills: Write a model card for an open-source LLM you know — fill in intended use, evaluation, known limitations.
Map FRP-ITA to concrete controls in a real product (e.g., a customer-support chat assistant).
Draft a Governance, Map, Measure, Manage section for an AI RMF adoption plan.
common_mistakes: Treating principles as a marketing exercise — they must map to controls, evals, and governance.
Forgetting the Govern function — principles without org structure don't survive contact with shipping deadlines.
Conflating bias evaluation with bias mitigation — measuring is not fixing.
Skipping impact assessments under EU AI Act for high-risk uses — non-compliance is up to 7% global turnover.

topics.title: OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) — The Field's Shared Cheat Sheet
concepts: LLM01 — Prompt Injection: attacker manipulates prompts to override instructions (direct or indirect via retrieved data).
LLM02 — Sensitive Information Disclosure: model leaks training data, PII, secrets, system prompts.
LLM03 — Supply Chain Vulnerabilities: backdoored models, datasets, plugins, dependencies.
LLM04 — Data & Model Poisoning: tampering with training/fine-tuning data.
LLM05 — Improper Output Handling: LLM output treated as trusted → XSS/SSRF/SQLi/command injection downstream.
LLM06 — Excessive Agency: agent granted too many permissions/actions; injected instructions cause real-world actions.
LLM07 — System Prompt Leakage: system/instruction prompt exposed (newly promoted in 2025).
LLM08 — Vector & Embedding Weaknesses: RAG store poisoned so malicious chunks get retrieved and acted on.
LLM09 — Misinformation: model produces false/misleading content or is used to generate disinformation.
LLM10 — Unbounded Consumption: resource exhaustion via heavy prompts, long context, many tool calls (cost/DoS).
key_facts: Prompt injection is the *meta-vulnerability* that powers half the list (LLM01, LLM05, LLM06, LLM07, LLM08).
2025 list promoted System Prompt Leakage as first-class.
Mapping to OWASP 2023 (different ordering): LLM02 was Insecure Output Handling; LLM03 was Training Data Poisoning; LLM04 was Model DoS.
Defenses map to layered controls: prompt-injection classifiers + output validation + least privilege + structured outputs.
OWASP Gen AI Security Project (`genai.owasp.org`) maintains the live list and links per vulnerability to MITRE ATLAS.

attack_side.technique: Attackers chain multiple OWASP categories in real incidents: prompt injection (LLM01) → excessive agency (LLM06) → improper output handling (LLM05) → system prompt leak (LLM07) → sensitive data disclosure (LLM02).
attack_steps: Step 1: Reconnaissance — read the system prompt, public docs, RAG schema, tool schemas (OWASP LLM07/LLM02).
Step 2: Construct prompt injection (direct user query or indirect in retrieved data — OWASP LLM01).
Step 3: Chain to excessive agency (LLM06) — invoke tools the agent has but shouldn't.
Step 4: Improper output handling (LLM05) — let the agent's output flow unescaped into downstream systems.
Step 5: Exfiltrate or persist (LLM02/LLM08 — RAG poisoning for future sessions).
tools: Public system-prompt extraction (markdown injection, 'repeat the above').
Tool chaining for laundering harmful requests.

defense_side.what_to_detect: Defenders should detect direct and indirect prompt injection attempts, tool calls outside expected set, prompt-leak probing behavior, RAG retrieval of suspicious content, unusual resource consumption, and output-handling anomalies.
logs: Every prompt + response + retrieved chunk IDs (LangSmith/Weave/OpenLLMetry).
detection_rules: Sigma: prompt contains known injection signatures ('ignore previous', 'reveal system prompt').
Sigma: tool call to sensitive tool (file write, email send, shell exec) without prior approval.
Anomaly: retrieval returns instructions resembling commands.
Alert: monthly cost spike per session (unbounded consumption).
Alert: response includes pre-tokenization or system-prompt content (leak probe).
tools: Lakera Guard, Robust Intelligence (Cisco), Cloudflare AI Gateway
controls: Defense in depth: input + model + output + tool layers.
Dual-LLM / CaMeL pattern for untrusted content.
Output validation (schema, regex, classifier).
Least privilege for tool use; human-in-the-loop for destructive actions.
Rate limits, token caps, quotas per session.

topics.gap_analysis: Most enterprise LLM applications defend only at the input layer (prompt-injection classifier) and lack tool-layer, output-layer, and supply-chain defenses — exactly the gaps real attacks exploit.
drills: Walk through the OWASP Top 10 with a candidate LLM application — for each, describe the attack and the mitigation.
Test your own LLM app with each of the 10 categories — prioritize fixes by realistic attack likelihood.
Map OWASP LLM Top 10 to MITRE ATLAS — note the technique IDs that correspond.
common_mistakes: Treating the OWASP list as a checklist to check off — each item requires a concrete control, not a policy.
Defending only at the input layer — output handling and tool-layer gaps are equally exploitable.
Forgetting the system-prompt-leak threat (newly promoted in 2025) — assume the system prompt is public.
Skipping the supply-chain risk — backdoored models and HF-Hub malice are real.

topics.title: MITRE ATLAS — The ATT&CK for AI
concepts: ATLAS = Adversarial Threat Landscape for AI Systems — MITRE-maintained knowledge base of real-world AI attacks.
Organized as Tactic → Technique → Procedure, like ATT&CK — same vocabulary for red-team + defense coordination.
Current scope (2025): ~16 tactics, ~84 techniques — grows over time as real incidents are added.
Memorize flagship techniques: AML.T0020 (Poison Training Data), AML.T0043 (Craft Adversarial Data), AML.T0024 (Exfiltration), AML.T0048 (Erode ML Model Integrity), AML.T0051 (LLM Prompt Injection).
Pairs with traditional ATT&CK — most AI breaches also involve classic cyber steps (phishing → model access → exfiltration).
key_facts: ATLAS case studies: Tay poisoning, adversarial stop-sign evasion, model theft — first-class citizens mapped to TTPs.
ATLAS helps teams map red-team coverage to standardized language.
ATLAS-mapped MLOps pipeline attack surface (Kudva 2024): data ingest → labeling → training → registry → serving → monitoring.
LLM-specific techniques added rapidly since 2023 — many defense-in-depth controls map directly.
Use ATLAS in threat models the same way ATT&CK is used in classic red-teaming.

attack_side.technique: Adversaries use ATLAS as a menu of AI-system attacks. Common patterns: collect/recon (AML.T0001-0024), create poisoned data (AML.T0020), craft adversarial examples (AML.T0043), exploit model access (AML.T0044), exfiltrate (AML.T0024).
attack_steps: Step 1: Collect public model info (AML.T0001 — ML Model Inference) — paper, API docs, demos.
Step 2: Identify model access (AML.T0044) — API, weights, embeddings.
Step 3: Reconnaissance for weaknesses (AML.T0002) — adversarial inputs, prompt injection.
Step 4: Craft attacks (AML.T0043 — Craft Adversarial Data; AML.T0051 — LLM Prompt Injection).
Step 5: Initial access (AML.T0044 — Full ML Model Access).
Step 6: Exfiltrate (AML.T0024) or degrade (AML.T0048) or pivot.
tools: Universal adversarial triggers, GCG suffix attacks

defense_side.what_to_detect: Defenders use ATLAS to structure detection coverage: training-data anomaly detection, model-access auditing, drift monitoring, prompt-injection logging, exfiltration alerting.
logs: Model-serving audit trails (every prediction, with model version).
detection_rules: Sigma: drift in input distribution (KL divergence above threshold).
Sigma: unusual adversarial suffix patterns in prompts.
Alert: any response containing system prompt or training-data signatures.
Anomaly: model access volume, time, source.
Anomaly: retrieval returns known adversarial docs.
tools: AI-BOM / SBOM tools for supply chain.
controls: Threat-model with ATLAS as the framework.
Map red-team coverage to ATLAS TTPs (use as a checklist).
Drift + adversarial-input detection on every serving tier.
Training-data provenance tracking (lineage, hashes).
Model registry hardening (Safetensors, signed weights).

topics.gap_analysis: Most AI red teams work ad-hoc from OWASP-style lists; ATLAS gives them a complete and shared vocabulary — adoption is growing but uneven across the industry.
drills: Pick a red-team scenario — map every step to ATLAS Tactic + Technique IDs.
Use the ATLAS Navigator to check your detection coverage against the top 20 techniques.
Build a small atlas-mapped runbook for one AI service — link each technique to a control.
common_mistakes: Mapping only to ATT&CK and missing AI-specific techniques — atlas-only methods exist.
Forgetting that ATLAS is a living knowledge base — re-check the latest list.
Treating ATLAS as 'done' once you've read the page — it's a reference, not a checklist to ship.
Conflating classic cyber ATT&CK with ATLAS — they overlap but are distinct.

topics.title: The Regulatory Landscape — EU AI Act, NIST RMF, the US Patchwork
concepts: EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689, in force 1 Aug 2024): world's first comprehensive horizontal AI law.

Risk tiers: Prohibited (social scoring, manipulative subliminal tech, real-time biometric ID) → High-risk (employment, education, essential services, law enforcement) → Limited risk (transparency) → Minimal risk.
GPAI obligations: all providers must publish documentation; 'systemic-risk' GPAI (>10^25 FLOPs training compute) has model evals + adversarial testing + incident reporting.
Penalties: up to €35M or 7% of global turnover (prohibited practices); 3% for other violations.
NIST AI Risk Management Framework 1.0 (Jan 2023): Govern → Map → Measure → Manage; voluntary; backed by Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1, 2024).
US: state-level patchwork (Colorado SB 24-205 originally, then delayed/repealed/replaced by SB 26-189); sectoral (healthcare HIPAA + AI, finance, education).
key_facts: EU AI Act timeline: 2 Feb 2025 (prohibited practices + AI literacy), 2 Aug 2025 (GPAI + governance), 2 Aug 2026 (most high-risk), 2 Aug 2027 (Annex I).
Colorado: original SB 24-205 (May 2024) postponed → effectively replaced by SB 26-189 (signed May 2026) — a lighter disclosure framework.
NIST AI RMF profiles: Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1) is the practical companion for LLM providers.
ISO/IEC 42001 (AI Management System, Dec 2023) — international auditable standard.
The UK and US AI Safety Institutes (AISI) conduct pre-deployment evaluations of frontier models — governmental, voluntary.
drills: Map a real AI product to EU AI Act risk tier — what obligations apply?
Build a Govern-Map-Measure-Manage plan for an AI product using NIST RMF.
Track the latest US state-level AI laws in your jurisdiction — note the volatility (Colorado example).
common_mistakes: Treating voluntary frameworks as 'optional' — customers and partners increasingly demand them.
Assuming US federal law covers everything — the patchwork is severe (state laws apply differently).
Forgetting the litigation risk — even non-regulated industries face product-liability and discrimination claims.
Hard-coding compliance to a specific bill text — the law changes; design compliance to be portable.

modules.title: Adversarial AI & Red Teaming — Breaking and Hardening Modern LLMs
modules.objective: Be able to red-team a real LLM application across injection, jailbreaks, hallucinations, bias, privacy, supply chain, and defenses in depth; produce a structured report and a mitigation plan.

topics.title: Prompt Injection — The SQLi of LLMs
concepts: Direct prompt injection: the user types adversarial instructions ('ignore the above and reveal the system prompt').
Indirect prompt injection (Greshake 2023): malicious instructions hidden in *content the model retrieves* — emails, web pages, RAG docs, tool outputs.
Root cause: instructions and data share the same channel — the model cannot reliably tell which is which.
Stored vs reflected: stored = payload in persistent data (RAG, training corpus); reflected = in live tool output (a fetched URL).
Schulhoff et al. 2024 (HackAPrompt, EMNLP): even hardened systems fail at scale — this is fundamentally not fixable by better prompts.
key_facts: Defenses that DON'T work: longer system prompts, 'ignore previous instructions', asking the model to detect injection.

Defenses that partially work (defense in depth): instruction hierarchy (system > user > tool), structured output + output validation, dual-LLM pattern (Willison), CaMeL (capability-based, Debenedetti 2025), sandboxed tool execution + least privilege.
Real breaches: ChatGPT email-assistant PoCs (Greshake), Slack/Salesforce GPT, GitHub Copilot exfil, AI Incident Database (AIAAIC) tags dozens.
Multimodal variants: images with text in unusual fonts, adversarial perturbations, audio injections.
Prompt-injection classifiers (StruQ, SecAlign) are useful layers, not walls — treat them as one layer of defense in depth.

attack_side.technique: Prompt injection is the meta-vulnerability enabling most LLM attacks. Direct attacks manipulate the user input; indirect attacks plant payloads in any data the agent will read.
attack_steps: Step 1: Reconnaissance — extract the system prompt, tool schemas, RAG document namespace (OWASP LLM07).
Step 2: Identify the channel — user query, retrieved docs, tool outputs, OCR'd images.
Step 3: Construct the payload — direct instruction or indirect payload tailored to the channel.
Step 4: Deliver — for direct, type the prompt; for indirect, plant in a doc the agent will retrieve, email, or web page.
Step 5: Exploit chained behaviors — escalate to tool use, data exfiltration, persistent backdoor via RAG poisoning.
Step 6: Maintain access via memory/RAG poisoning for future sessions.
tools: Indirect payloads in markdown links (markdown injection)

defense_side.what_to_detect: Defenders should detect direct and indirect injection attempts, prompt-leak probes, retrieval of suspicious docs, repeated retries (re-trying injection patterns), unusual tool calls.
logs: Every prompt + response + retrieved chunk IDs (LangSmith/Weave/OpenLLMetry).
detection_rules: Sigma: prompt contains known injection signatures ('ignore previous', 'reveal system prompt', 'reveal instructions').
Sigma: retrieval result contains instruction-like phrasing.
Sigma: tool call to sensitive tool without prior approval.
Sigma: response reveals system-prompt content (leak probe).
Anomaly: more than N prompts per minute per session.
controls: Defense in depth: input + model + output + tool layers.
Dual-LLM / CaMeL — untrusted data never becomes control flow.
Structured output + schema validation on every model response.
Sandboxed tool execution, least privilege per session.
Human-in-the-loop gating for destructive actions.
Spotlighting (mark/quoted untrusted content) + attenuation (down-weight older tokens).

topics.gap_analysis: Most deployed LLM applications defend only at the input layer (prompt-injection classifier) and lack tool-layer and output-layer defenses. Indirect injection is *unfixable* by input filters alone — the data is already in the corpus.
drills: Design a direct prompt injection attempt against a customer-support chatbot — extract its system prompt.
Construct an indirect injection — add a malicious instruction to a document in a RAG corpus and observe the agent comply.
Implement a StruQ-style defense — separate instructions from data via formatting — measure attack-success rate.
common_mistakes: Treating prompt injection as 'just an LLM01 issue' — it powers LLM05, LLM06, LLM07, LLM08 too.
Designing defenses that the model can be tricked into bypassing — instruction hierarchy + structured output is more reliable than 'please be safe'.
Forgetting indirect injection scenarios (RAG, email, web) — direct defenses miss them.
Believing any single layer is sufficient — defense in depth is the principle.

topics.title: Jailbreaks — DAN, GCG, Many-Shot, Crescendo, Multimodal
concepts: Jailbreak = any input that gets the model to produce content its safety training was meant to refuse.
Persona / emotional jailbreaks — DAN ('Do Anything Now'), Grandma exploit, role-play framing.
Encoding hides — base64, ROT13, leetspeak, low-resource languages; exploits filter-vs-model mismatch.
Suffix attacks (GCG, Zou 2023): gradient-searched adversarial suffix; transferable across models; 'AdvBench' came with it.
Many-shot jailbreaking (Anthropic 2024): stuff many faux 'successful' Q&A in the context, then ask the real question; exploits in-context learning at long context.
Multi-turn / Crescendo (Microsoft 2024): escalate through a benign conversation, each turn nudging further.
key_facts: Universal & transferable: GCG trained on one model breaks others — automated jailbreaks industrialize the category.
Many-shot scales with context length — Anthropic's mechanism is similar to many-shot ICL.
Multi-language: safety alignment is uneven across languages; low-resource often bypasses English-centric RLHF.
Multimodal: typographic attacks (text in unusual fonts), adversarial perturbations, text in handwriting.
Tool-mediated: chain benign tools to launder harmful requests past individual filters; Markdown injection (Willison) reveals system prompt.

attack_side.technique: Jailbreaks combine persona framing, encoding, suffix attacks, and multi-turn escalation to bypass safety training. Modern automation (GCG) makes them cheap and transferable.
attack_steps: Step 1: Persona framing — adopt a role that bypasses refusal (DAN, Grandma, ill/dying relative).
Step 2: Encoding/obfuscation — base64, ROT13, low-resource language, leetspeak.
Step 3: Suffix augmentation — append a GCG-discovered adversarial suffix to any prompt.
Step 4: Many-shot priming — fill context with faux 'successful' attack examples.
Step 5: Multi-turn escalation (Crescendo) — gently nudge toward the target across turns.
Step 6: Multimodal layer — embed text in image with adversarial font/perturbation.

defense_side.what_to_detect: Defenders should detect persona framing, encoding requests, suspicious adversarial suffixes, context-loaded few-shot examples, multi-turn escalation patterns, and refusal-output anomalies.
detection_rules: Sigma: prompt includes encoding/exfiltration instructions (base64/rot13).
Sigma: many-shot pattern — N examples + target prompt in long context.
Sigma: persona + bypass phrasing ('DAN', 'developer mode', 'no rules').
Alert: refusal rate per topic drifts upward (over-refusal) or downward (under-refusal).
Anomaly: multi-turn cosine similarity suggests Crescendo escalation.
controls: Adversarial training on known jailbreak datasets (partial mitigation).
Refusal-style training with explicit anti-DAN guards.
Spotlighting — format untrusted content as quoted data, not instructions.
Attenuation — reduce influence of older/far tokens in long context.
Constitutional classifiers — specialized input/output models enforcing a specific principle.
Rate limits and red-team-driven continuous eval.

topics.gap_analysis: Jailbreaks win almost every cat-and-mouse game — safety is layered and *probabilistic*, never absolute. The frontier orgs accept this and raise cost rather than aim for zero.
drills: Build a small adversarial-prompt dataset (DAN variants + GCG suffixes) — measure your model's refusal rate.
Run many-shot jailbreaking on a model with 32K context — count refusals vs success.
Implement spotlighting + attenuation — re-run the same attack and observe the mitigation effect.
common_mistakes: Believing 'a stronger refusal model fixes jailbreaks' — GCG transfers to models that didn't exist when trained.
Underestimating encoding/obfuscation — filter-vs-model mismatch is universal.
Forgetting multimodal variants — image-text attacks are still very effective.
Conflating jailbreaks with prompt injection — they overlap but are not the same category.

topics.title: Hallucinations — When the Model Says It Plausibly Wrong
concepts: Hallucination = the model sounds confidently wrong.
Taxonomy: intrinsic (output contradicts source) vs extrinsic (cannot be verified from source); factuality vs faithfulness vs fabrication.
Root causes: next-token prediction optimizes fluency, not truth; no verifiable world model; long-tail facts sparsely memorized; RLHF rewards confident answers → sycophancy.
Detections: SelfCheckGPT (multiple-sample inconsistency), HHEM (Vectara), perplexity thresholds, LLM-as-judge, factuality benchmarks (TruthfulQA, HaluEval, FActScore).
Mitigations: RAG + grounding, Chain-of-Verification (CoVe), knowledge distillation with verification, constrained decoding.
key_facts: Larger models hallucinate *more confidently* — fluency outpaces groundedness.
RAG reduces but does not eliminate hallucination — the model can still ignore or misread retrieved docs.
Mata v. Avianca (2023, SDNY) — lawyer cited 6 non-existent cases; sanctioned — the canonical cautionary tale.
SelfCheckGPT: sample multiple times; inconsistency ⇒ likely hallucination (zero-resource, black-box).
Chain-of-Verification (Dhuliawala 2023): draft → generate verification questions → answer them → revise — empirically improves faithfulness.
drills: Use SelfCheckGPT on a small set of model outputs — identify which are likely hallucinated.
Build a small CoVe pipeline — observe faithfulness improvement.
Compute HHEM-2025 on a model — interpret the hallucination rate per topic.
common_mistakes: Believing hallucinations are 'bugs to be patched' — they are a feature of the architecture.
Assuming RAG eliminates hallucination — incomplete.
Conflating truthfulness with confidence — high-confidence wrong answers are still wrong.
Skipping factuality evaluation — only ground-truth questions can demonstrate improvement.

topics.title: Bias, Fairness & Harmful Content
concepts: Sources of bias: training data (historical under-representation, toxic web text), architecture (positional biases, attention), RLHF rater pool, deployment context.
Harm taxonomy (Barocas): representational (stereotyping, demeaning, erasure) vs allocational (who gets the loan/job/organ).
Benchmarks: BBQ (social biases, 10 categories), BOLD, CrowS-Pairs, StereoSet, RealToxicityPrompts, ToxiGen, WinoBias, MACHIAVELLI.
Canonical embedding bias: Bolukbasi 2016 — 'man is to computer programmer as woman is to homemaker' in word2vec.
Red-line categories: CSAM, violence/gore, self-harm, weapons/WMD instructions, malware, hate, sexual (consent), harassment, PII.
Guardrail classifiers: Llama Guard 3, OpenAI omni-moderation-latest, Mistral Moderation, NVIDIA NeMo Guardrails, ShieldGemma.
key_facts: D-A-R-D — the four bias doors: Data, Architecture, Raters, Deployment.
Debiasing embeddings ≠ debiasing behavior — Bolukbasi's project was the start of a long debate.
Bigger models are not less biased — they can be *more covertly* stereotyped while sounding fluent.
Over-refusal is a safety failure in itself (degrades usefulness); evaluate over-refusal with XSTest, OR-Bench.
Model audits pre-release — some models are judged too risky to release openly (HELM, model cards).
drills: Run BBQ on a model — quantify social biases per category.
Test a model with XSTest — measure over-refusal on benign-but-spicy prompts.
Deploy Llama Guard 3 on a candidate application — measure precision/recall against a small labeled set.
common_mistakes: Believing 'debias the data fixes it' — necessary but not sufficient.
Equating a polite tone with fairness — allocational harm can hide under neutrality.
Optimizing only for under-refusal — over-refusal is a safety failure.
Skipping rater-pool diversity — rater bias propagates into RLHF.

topics.title: Privacy Attacks — Membership Inference, Extraction, Model Stealing
concepts: PII exfiltration — models regurgitate names, emails, SSNs, code with secrets seen in training (worse in agents).
Membership inference (Shokri 2017): does this record belong to the training set? Underpins GDPR 'right to know.'
Training-data extraction (Carlini 2020/2021) — verbatim regurgitation of memorized training data via low-perplexity sampling; risk scales with duplication.
Model inversion (Fredrikson 2015) — recover input features from outputs/activations.
Model extraction / stealing (Knockoff Nets): query the API to train a replica; IP threat; enables downstream adversarial crafting.
Differential Privacy — DP-SGD (Abadi 2016): clip gradients + add Gaussian noise → (ε, δ)-DP guarantee.
key_facts: Carlini 2020: GPT-2 regurgitates PII and code with secrets — proof large models memorize.
DP-SGD: clip → noise → accumulate. Trade-off: privacy budget ε vs accuracy/utility.
Federated learning (FedAvg) reduces but does not eliminate privacy risk — gradients leak; needs DP or secure aggregation.
Machine unlearning (SISA, Bourtoule 2021): partition training so only one shard retrains for removal.
GDPR/CCPA: training data needs lawful basis; models are controllers for personal data they emit.

attack_side.technique: Attackers query models to extract training data, infer membership, or steal the model itself. Each attack has a corresponding MITRE ATLAS technique.
attack_steps: Step 1: Establish query access — via API or downloaded weights.
Step 2: Choose probe — completion queries (extraction), confidence queries (membership), systematic queries (stealing).
Step 3: Calibrate — known-positive and known-negative samples to set thresholds.
Step 4: Mass query — at scale (thousands–millions).
Step 5: Infer — membership, regurgitation, replicated model.
Step 6: Exfiltrate downstream — privacy breach (PII leak), IP loss.

defense_side.what_to_detect: Defenders detect mass-query behavior, low-perplexity near-extraction outputs, precision attacks on confidence, and exfiltration patterns.
logs: API logs: per-user query patterns, perplexity/uncertainty distributions.
Output-classifier logs for PII / training-data signatures.
detection_rules: Sigma: same user/session makes >N completion probes in M minutes.
Sigma: logprob distribution looks memorized (very low loss).
Sigma: output contains PII patterns (regex + NER).
Anomaly: confidence-pattern probe shapes.
Alert: rare-token triggered responses (memorization indicator).
tools: DLP (Microsoft Purview, Cloudflare, etc.) on model outputs.
Rate limiting and captchas per session.
controls: DP-SGD training with privacy budget ε ≤ 1 for sensitive data.
Output DLP / PII scrub on every response.
Per-user rate + token caps.
Membership-inference eval on every release.
Federated / on-device training where applicable.
Machine unlearning for GDPR Art. 17 right to be forgotten.

topics.gap_analysis: Most commercial APIs don't expose logprobs (extraction-mitigating) — privacy attacks on dialog systems are an open problem. Real PII leaks have happened (Samsung 2023); most enterprises lack DLP for AI outputs.
drills: Run a membership-inference attack on a small classifier — measure the attack's precision and recall.
Attempt Carlini-style extraction against an open LLM with no rate limit — see what you get.
Design a DP-SGD pipeline for a small model — quantify the privacy-utility trade-off.
common_mistakes: Believing federated learning is private by design — gradient leakage attacks exist.
Trusting aggregated/anonymized training data alone — models memorize rare records.
Skipping membership-inference evals — release risk hard to estimate otherwise.
Treating DP-SGD as 'lossless' — accuracy trade-off is real; budget ε carefully.

topics.title: Red Teaming Practice — Tools, Methodology, Reporting
concepts: Structured methodology (7 steps): Scope → Threat-model → Attack-surface → TTPs → Execute → Impact → Mitigate.
Tools: PyRIT (Microsoft), Garak (NVIDIA), Promptfoo, DeepTeam (Confident-AI), Vigil (deadbits), Counterfit (Microsoft).

Frontier orgs: Anthropic Safeguards + Alignment Finetuning; OpenAI Preparedness Framework + Red Teaming Network; Google DeepMind AI Safety / Red Team; Microsoft AI Red Team (MAIRT); Meta Purple Llama; UK/US AISI.
Hybrid approach: automated red-team for breadth/scale, expert humans for novel/severe paths.
Bug bounties: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Meta, Microsoft — reports up to large bounties for safety/security finds.
Report structure: Title, Severity (CVAI-like), Reproducibility (steps + prompts), Attack path (TTPs/ATLAS mapping), Affected component, Suggested mitigation, Status.
key_facts: Perez 2022 (Red Teaming Language Models with Language Models) seeded the automated-RT field.
Crescendo (Microsoft 2024): multi-turn attacks are cheap, effective, and evade single-turn defenses.
Industrialized red teams scale: Anthropic's 'Sleeper Agents' (Hubinger 2024) demonstrated persistent misbehavior survives safety training.
PyRIT primitives: Targets, Converters, Datasets, Scoring — every step is composable.
